column3: Mr. Z, a legal practitioner, is asked by a client to represent him in a criminal trial for armed robbery. The client admits to Mr. Z in private that he actually committed the robbery but wants Z to plead "not guilty" and argue his innocence in court. What should Mr. Z do?
column4: He must withdraw immediately because he cannot represent a guilty person.
column5: He can plead "not guilty" but cannot call false evidence or argue a positive defence he knows to be false.
column6: He must report the confession to the judge.
column7: He must convince the client to plead "guilty" or he will face professional discipline.
column9: A confidential disclosure of guilt does not require withdrawal, but under Rule 15(3)(f), a lawyer cannot knowingly use perjured evidence or a false defence; he may however test the prosecution's case to see if it meets the burden of proof [9, 10].

column3: Alhaji Musa, a billionaire, wants to hire a top-tier lawyer, Mrs. Williams, to take instructions at his private residence because he is "too busy" to visit a law office. Musa is in perfect health and is not under any legal restriction. What is the ethical position for Mrs. Williams?
column4: She should go to his house since he is a high-paying client.
column5: She should not call at the client's house for advice or instructions unless there are special circumstances or urgency.
column6: She can go if she takes her secretary along as a witness.
column7: She must only meet him in a hotel lobby to avoid violating the rules.
column9: Rule 22 of the RPC states that a lawyer should not call at a client's house or place of business for advice or instructions except in special circumstances like extreme old age, infirmity, or custody [11, 12].

column3: Barrister Tolu collects 15 million Naira as a judgment debt on behalf of his client. Since he is currently renovating his own house and needs urgent cash, he deposits the money into his personal savings account, intending to pay the client back after his next big fee comes in. What duty has he breached?
column4: Duty of competence
column5: Duty of dedication
column6: Duty to deal with client's property honestly and separately
column7: Duty to accept brief
column9: Rule 23(2) of the RPC provides that a lawyer who collects money for his client shall not mix such money with his own and must report and account for it promptly [11, 13].

column3: A legal practitioner, Mr. Effiong, is representing a plaintiff in a land suit. Halfway through the trial, Effiong realizes that he was a witness to the original survey agreement and his testimony is essential to prove the case. What is the proper course of action?
column4: He should continue as counsel and also testify as a witness.
column5: He should withdraw from the case and let another lawyer handle the trial while he testifies as a witness.
column6: He should refuse to testify to protect the attorney-client relationship.
column7: He should ask the judge to decide based on his oral arguments without his testimony.
column9: Rule 20 of the RPC provides that a lawyer should not accept employment if he knows he or a lawyer in his firm may be a witness; if he discovers this during trial, he should withdraw from the case [14, 15].

column3: Chief Benson retains Barrister Amina for a civil litigation matter. After two hearings, Benson informs Amina that he is changing his lawyer to Barrister Kunle. Amina refuses to hand over the case file until her outstanding fees are fully paid. Is she within her rights?
column4: No, she must hand over everything to the new lawyer immediately.
column5: Yes, a lawyer has a lien on the papers or documents of his client in respect of unpaid fees.
column6: No, she can only withhold the client's original title deeds but not the correspondence.
column7: Yes, but only if she gets a court order to withhold the files.
column9: Under Rule 29(3)(c) of the RPC, the former lawyer is entitled to a lien on the papers or documents of his client in respect of unpaid fees [16, 17].

column3: A new legal practitioner, Joy, is approached to handle a complex international oil and gas arbitration. Joy has no experience in this field and knows she is not competent to handle it alone. What should she do to comply with ethical rules?
column4: Decline the brief immediately.
column5: Accept the brief and learn on the job using AI tools.
column6: Handle the matter but associate with a lawyer who is competent in that field, unless the client objects.
column7: Charge a lower fee to compensate for her lack of experience.
column9: Rule 16(1)(a) of the RPC prohibits a lawyer from handling a legal matter they are not competent to handle without associating with a lawyer who is competent, unless the client objects [18, 19].

column3: Barrister Segun is representing a client in a debt recovery suit. During the pendency of the case, Segun enters into an agreement with the client to buy the very property that is the subject of the litigation. Does this constitute professional misconduct?
column4: No, as long as he pays the market value for the property.
column5: Yes, a lawyer shall not acquire proprietary interest in the subject matter of litigation he is conducting.
column6: No, because it ensures he is motivated to win the case.
column7: Yes, unless the opposing counsel also agrees to the sale.
column9: Rule 17(3) of the RPC provides that a lawyer shall not acquire proprietary interest in the cause of action or subject matter of litigation which he is conducting for a client [20].

column3: A client instructs his lawyer, Barrister Eze, to prepare a deed of assignment for a land transaction. The client explicitly tells Eze to "backdate" the document by two years to avoid a new tax law. Eze complies. What is the ethical implication?
column4: Eze has acted correctly by following his client's instructions.
column5: Eze has breached his duty to represent the client within the bounds of the law.
column6: Eze is protected by attorney-client privilege.
column7: Eze has only committed a minor clerical error, not misconduct.
column9: Rule 15(1) and (2) of the RPC state that a lawyer's first duty is to uphold the law and he must keep strictly within the law notwithstanding any contrary instruction from the client [21, 22].

column3: Barrister Kemi represents a defendant in a criminal case. She enters into an agreement with the client that she will only be paid if the client is acquitted. If the client is found guilty, she gets nothing. Is this arrangement valid?
column4: Yes, this is a valid contingent fee arrangement.
column5: No, a lawyer shall not enter into an arrangement to charge or collect a contingent fee for representing a defendant in a criminal case.
column6: Yes, provided the agreement is in writing.
column7: No, unless the judge approves the success fee in advance.
column9: Rule 50(2) of the RPC specifically prohibits a lawyer from entering into an arrangement to charge or collect a contingent fee for representing a defendant in a criminal case [23, 24].

column3: A client informs his lawyer, Mr. Wale, that he has just committed a massive fraud against a federal agency. Wale has already finished the work for this client and the relationship has ended. Can Wale reveal this information to the agency?
column4: Yes, because fraud is a crime.
column5: No, because the communication was made in the course of professional employment and is privileged.
column6: Yes, because the relationship has ended and privilege no longer applies.
column7: No, unless the agency pays Wale a reward for the information.
column9: Rule 19 of the RPC and Section 192 of the Evidence Act protect communications made in the course of professional employment; privilege continues even after the relationship has terminated [25-27]."

column3: Barrister Ngozi is representing a client in a case against "Company A." She later discovers that her law partner, Mr. John, is currently representing "Company A" in an unrelated tax matter. What is the ethical requirement here?
column4: They can continue as long as they don't discuss the case.
column5: Ngozi must disclose the conflict to the client and obtain consent, as the rules on conflict of interest extend to partners.
column6: Only Ngozi needs to withdraw; Mr. John can continue.
column7: No disclosure is needed since the two matters are unrelated.
column9: Rule 17(1) and (6) of the RPC require disclosure of interests that might influence the client's selection of the lawyer, and these rules extend to the lawyer's partners and associates [20, 28, 29].

column3: A legal practitioner, Mr. Adams, is assigned a pro bono case by the Legal Aid Council. Adams decides not to prepare for the case or attend the trial since he is not being paid. What rule has he breached?
column4: Rule 24 on the Cab Rank Rule
column5: Rule 52 on undercutting
column6: Rule 14 on dedication and Rule 16 on competence
column7: Rule 7 on engaging in business
column9: Under Rule 14 and 16, a lawyer must devote expertise and energy to the service of his client; Section 9(1) of the LPA makes a lawyer liable for negligence regardless of whether services are provided for a fee or pro bono [1, 2, 17, 30].

column3: During a trial, Barrister Ibrahim finds a recent Supreme Court decision that completely destroys his client's case. The opposing counsel is a junior lawyer who is unaware of this decision. What is Ibrahim's duty?
column4: He must remain silent and hope the other side doesn't find it.
column5: He must disclose the authority to the court, even though it is against his case.
column6: He should withdraw from the case immediately.
column7: He should tell the opposing counsel about it in private but not the judge.
column9: Rule 32 of the RPC (and Rule 37) requires a lawyer to be candid and fair; he must not fail to cite a decided case that is against him which has not been cited by the opposing counsel [31-33].

column3: Barrister Sola is approached by a client to help recover a debt. Sola tells the client, "I will definitely win this case for you; there is no way we can lose." Has Sola acted properly?
column4: Yes, it builds the client's confidence.
column5: No, a lawyer should refrain from making bold and confident assurances, especially since the other side hasn't been heard.
column6: Yes, provided he actually wins.
column7: No, unless he puts the guarantee in writing.
column9: Under established ethics of the profession, where litigation is advised, counsel should refrain from making bold and confident assurances to the client [34].

column3: A legal practitioner, Mrs. Idowu, is representing a client in a civil suit. She forgets the trial date and fails to attend court, leading to the case being struck out. When the client sues her for negligence, she points to a clause in their retainer agreement that says, "the lawyer shall not be liable for any mistakes made during the case." Is this clause valid?
column4: Yes, parties are free to contract as they wish.
column5: No, any provision purporting to exclude or limit a lawyer's liability for negligence in a contract is void.
column6: Yes, but only if the client signed it before a Notary Public.
column7: No, unless the NBA President approved the waiver.
column9: Section 9(1) of the Legal Practitioners Act (LPA) provides that a person shall not be immune from liability for damages attributable to his negligence, and any contract provision purporting to exclude this liability is void [30, 35].

column3: Barrister Dele is retained by a client in a property matter. While the matter is ongoing, Dele discovers that the client is using his law firm to launder money from an illegal drug business. What must Dele do to comply with current Nigerian law and ethics?
column4: He must keep the information secret due to attorney-client privilege.
column5: He must withdraw his services and report the suspicious activities to the appropriate authorities like the EFCC/NFIU.
column6: He should ask for a 10% cut of the laundered money as a "risk fee."
column7: He should only tell his local NBA Chairman.
column9: Under the Money Laundering (Prevention and Prohibition) Act 2022 and Rule 57 of the RPC 2023, lawyers have an obligation to report suspicious transactions to the appropriate authorities, and privilege does not cover transactions in furtherance of an unlawful act [36-39].

column3: The National Executive Committee of the Nigerian Bar Association (NBA) is currently embroiled in a leadership crisis that has paralyzed the association's management. The Body of Benchers (BOB) seeks to intervene to ensure the association's affairs continue. Under what condition can the BOB legally appoint a caretaker committee for the NBA?
column4: Only if the AGF specifically requests it in writing
column5: If the NEC passes a resolution requesting the BOB to do so
column6: Only if the Supreme Court declares the current NEC illegal
column7: If the NBA fails to hold elections for more than 60 days
column9: According to the Body of Benchers functions, one circumstance for appointing a caretaker committee is when the NEC of the NBA passes a resolution requesting the BOB to appoint one to run the affairs of the Association [1-3].

column3: Mr. Kola, a legal practitioner, was found guilty of infamous conduct by the Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Committee (LPDC) for misappropriating client funds. Dissatisfied with the direction, he intends to appeal the decision. To which body should Mr. Kola's appeal be directed?
column4: The General Council of the Bar
column5: The Body of Benchers
column6: The Supreme Court of Nigeria
column7: The Court of Appeal
column9: By virtue of Section 12(7) of the Legal Practitioners Act and the decision in Nwalutu v. NBA, an appeal from the direction of the LPDC lies directly to the Supreme Court of Nigeria [4-6].

column3: Chief Okon, a retired Justice of the Court of Appeal, is approached by his nephew to represent him as lead counsel in a high-stakes land dispute at the State High Court. Chief Okon is still physically fit and willing to argue the case. Which of the following best describes Chief Okon's legal position?
column4: He can appear since he is a retired judicial officer and not a serving one
column5: He can appear provided he does not wear his wig and gown
column6: He is prohibited from acting as a legal practitioner before any court of law in Nigeria
column7: He can only appear if he is granted a special warrant by the CJN
column9: Under Section 292(2) of the 1999 Constitution and Rule 6(3) of the RPC, a judicial officer who has retired is prohibited for life from acting as a legal practitioner or advocate before any court of law or tribunal in Nigeria [7-9].

column3: A new private university in Nigeria has just graduated its first set of law students and is seeking to have its law faculty accredited so that the graduates can be admitted to the Nigerian Law School. Which body is primarily responsible for the accreditation of law faculties and the vocational training of these aspirants?
column4: The National Universities Commission (NUC) only
column5: The Council of Legal Education (CLE)
column6: The Body of Benchers
column7: The General Council of the Bar
column9: The Council of Legal Education, established by the Legal Education Act, is responsible for the legal education of aspirants to the Bar, including the accreditation of law faculties and the issuance of qualifying certificates [4, 10, 11].

column3: Mr. Emeka, a legal practitioner called to the Bar in 2020, has failed to pay his annual practicing fees for the year 2024 as of May 2024. He is briefed to appear in a fundamental rights enforcement matter fixed for hearing in June 2024. What is the effect of his non-payment on his appearance?
column4: He can appear provided he pays the fee before the next adjourned date
column5: He has no right of audience in court until the fees are paid
column6: He can appear but cannot claim professional fees from the client
column7: He is only allowed to appear in civil matters but not criminal ones
column9: Section 8(2) of the Legal Practitioners Act and Rule 9(1) of the RPC provide that a legal practitioner shall not have a right of audience in any court unless he has paid his practicing fees for that year, which must be paid by March 31st [12-15].

column3: The Federal Government intends to review the maximum charges legal practitioners can collect for the preparation of deeds and other land-related documents. Which body is statutorily empowered to make orders regulating the charges of legal practitioners?
column4: The Nigerian Bar Association
column5: The General Council of the Bar
column6: The Legal Practitioners Remuneration Committee
column7: The Legal Practitioners Privileges Committee
column9: Section 15(1) of the Legal Practitioners Act establishes the Legal Practitioners Remuneration Committee, which has the power to make orders regulating the charges of legal practitioners, including maximum charges for specific activities [16, 17].

column3: A Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN) is briefed to defend a client in a complex fraud case before the High Court of Lagos State. On the day of the hearing, the junior counsel in his firm is unavailable due to an emergency. Can the SAN proceed to conduct the trial alone?
column4: No, a SAN must always appear with a junior in all cases
column5: Yes, because the restriction on appearing alone applies only to civil cases
column6: Yes, provided he obtains the leave of the presiding judge
column7: No, he must wait for another SAN to join him
column9: Under the Senior Advocates of Nigeria (Privileges and Functions) Rules, a SAN is prohibited from appearing alone in civil cases but may appear as counsel in any criminal case in any court without another counsel [18-20].

column3: Alhaji Bature, a wealthy businessman, asks his personal secretary, who is not a lawyer but has a "diploma in law," to draft a Deed of Mortgage for a property transaction in exchange for a ?50,000 "processing fee." What is the legal implication of this act?
column4: It is valid since the secretary has a diploma in law
column5: It is an offence under the Legal Practitioners Act
column6: It is only an offence if the Deed is eventually registered
column7: It is permissible if a lawyer later "franks" the document for them
column9: Section 22(1)(d) of the Legal Practitioners Act provides that any person other than a legal practitioner who prepares any instrument relating to immovable property in expectation of a reward is guilty of an offence [21-24].

column3: A legal practitioner is aggrieved by a new rule included in the Rules of Professional Conduct (RPC) and wishes to challenge the authority of the body that enacted it. Which body is responsible for enacting and revising the RPC in Nigeria?
column4: The Body of Benchers
column5: The Nigerian Bar Association
column6: The General Council of the Bar
column7: The National Judicial Council
column9: The General Council of the Bar, established by Section 1 of the LPA, is responsible for enacting and revising the Rules of Professional Conduct for legal practitioners [25-27].

column3: Mr. Ade, a legal practitioner, is also a full-time salaried Director of Legal Services at a multinational oil company. The company is sued in a commercial dispute, and the CEO directs Mr. Ade to robe and represent the company in court to save costs. Can Mr. Ade comply with this directive?
column4: Yes, provided he is a qualified legal practitioner
column5: No, a lawyer in salaried employment cannot appear as an advocate for his employer
column6: Yes, but only if he appears with an external counsel
column7: No, unless the company is a government department
column9: Rule 8(1) of the RPC 2023 stipulates that a member of the Bar, while in salaried employment, shall not appear as an advocate in court for his employer, except where he is employed as a legal officer in a government department [28, 29].

column3: Following a controversial election, the Registered Trustees of the NBA are sued in court regarding the validity of the national officers' tenure. The defendants argue that the NBA itself should have been sued. What is the legal status of the NBA regarding its capacity to sue or be sued?
column4: It is a juristic person and must be sued in its own name
column5: It is a juridical entity and can only be sued through its registered trustees
column6: It is a government agency under the AGF's office
column7: It has absolute immunity from all legal proceedings
column9: The NBA is a juridical and not a juristic entity, meaning it cannot be sued in its own name but rather through its registered/incorporated trustees, as decided in Fawehinmi v. NBA [30-32].

column3: A Senior Advocate of Nigeria is approached to represent a client in an appeal against a conviction for stealing at the Magistrate Court. Can the SAN personally sign the notice of appeal and argue the case before the Magistrate? "
column4: Yes, because he is a legal practitioner of the highest distinction
column5: No, a SAN is prohibited from appearing or signing processes for use in an inferior court
column6: Yes, provided the case involves a novel point of law
column7: Only if he is accompanied by three junior lawyers
column9: Based on the decision in Registered Trustees of ECWA v. Ijesha and the SAN Rules, a SAN shall not appear in any inferior court (Magistrate court and below) nor file and sign processes to be used in such courts [18, 33-35].

column3: The Legal Practitioners Privileges Committee (LPPC) is considering withdrawing the rank of SAN from a holder who was recently convicted of a felony. Who presides over the meetings of the LPPC?
column4: The Attorney-General of the Federation
column5: The President of the Nigerian Bar Association
column6: The Chief Justice of Nigeria
column7: The President of the Court of Appeal
column9: Section 5(3) of the Legal Practitioners Act provides that the Legal Practitioners Privileges Committee shall consist of the Chief Justice of Nigeria as the Chairman [36, 37].

column3: Mr. Musa was called to the Bar in 1995. He has consistently paid his practicing fees but was recently convicted of an offence involving financial dishonesty. He has filed an appeal which is currently pending. Can he be sanctioned by the LPDC under Section 12(1)(b) of the LPA? "
column4: Yes, conviction by a court is sufficient
column5: No, because the appeal is still pending and has not been determined
column6: Yes, provided the NBA approves the sanction
column7: Only if the Supreme Court grants a warrant
column9: Section 12(5) of the LPA provides that a person shall not be deemed convicted for the purpose of professional discipline if an appeal is pending or the time for appeal has not elapsed [38, 39].

column3: A newly called lawyer, Miss Funmi, wants to open her law office in Lagos immediately after her call to bar. A senior colleague tells her she must serve five years of pupillage before practicing on her own. What is the current legal position?
column4: She must serve 3 years of pupillage under a SAN
column5: She can engage in private practice immediately after being called to the Bar
column6: She must work in the Ministry of Justice for 2 years first
column7: Pupillage is mandatory only for those who want to practice in Lagos
column9: The previous restriction requiring pupillage was under the Regulated and Other Professions (Private Practice Prohibition) Act, which has been repealed. Currently, every legal practitioner can engage in private practice immediately after being called to the bar [40-42].

column3: A legal practitioner, Mr. John, is a party to a civil suit involving his personal property. He wants to appear in court, robe, and conduct the case himself while also representing his business partner who is a co-defendant. What is the correct procedure?
column4: He can robe and represent both himself and his partner
column5: He can represent himself as a litigant in person but cannot robe or represent another party
column6: He must hire an external lawyer as he is prohibited from self-representation
column7: He can only robe if he is a SAN or a Life Bencher
column9: Under Rule 17(5) of the RPC and the decision in Fawehinmi v. NBA, a lawyer shall not appear for a client in a proceeding in which he is himself a party; as a litigant, he conducts his case in person and is disallowed from robing [43, 44].

column3: A group of lawyers are dissatisfied with the quorum at a recent meeting of the General Council of the Bar where a decision was made. According to the Legal Practitioners Act, what is the required quorum for a meeting of the Bar Council?
column4: Five members
column5: Eight members
column6: Ten members
column7: Fifteen members
column9: Section 1(2) of the Legal Practitioners Act stipulates that the quorum for the General Council of the Bar shall be eight members [45, 46].

column3: A non-lawyer prepares a Will for his dying uncle and collects a "consultation fee" of ?10,000. Is this an act of impersonation of a legal practitioner under the law?
column4: Yes, only lawyers can prepare Wills for reward
column5: No, the exclusive right to prepare documents for reward does not extend to Wills
column6: Yes, if the Will is eventually proved in probate
column7: No, provided he does not use the title "Solicitor"
column9: Section 22(4) of the LPA provides exceptions to the restriction on preparing instruments for reward, explicitly stating that "instrument" in this context does not include a Will [47, 48].

column3: Mrs. Zainab, a legal practitioner of 8 years standing, applies to be appointed as a Notary Public. She has paid her practicing fees for the last 5 years but missed the two years prior to that. Does she meet the practical requirements for appointment?
column4: Yes, 5 years of payment is sufficient
column5: No, she must have paid for 7 uninterrupted years
column6: Yes, provided the Chief Judge of her state attests to her
column7: No, because only SANs can be Notaries Public
column9: Typically in practice, for the CJN to appoint a legal practitioner as a Notary Public, they are required to have paid practicing fees for a period of 7 uninterrupted years [49-51].

column3: The National Judicial Council (NJC) receives a petition against a High Court Judge for gross misconduct. After investigation, the NJC finds the judge guilty. What is the extent of the NJC's power regarding the judge's removal?
column4: The NJC can directly dismiss the judge from office
column5: The NJC can only recommend the judge's removal to the President or Governor
column6: The NJC can only suspend the judge but cannot recommend removal
column7: The NJC's decision is final and requires no executive action
column9:
Under Section 292 of the Constitution, the NJC cannot remove a judicial officer but can only recommend such removal to the President or Governor, as the case may be [52-55].
week 3,Keeping Terms and Dinner Etiquette , Chidi
column11: arrives at the Law School Dining Hall at 4:50 PM for the first dining term. The Benchers have not yet processed in
column12: but the doors are being closed. What is the likely consequence for Chidi? "
column13: He will be allowed in because he arrived before 5:00 PM
column14: He will be disallowed from keeping the dining term because students must be seated by 4:30 PM
column15: He will be allowed in but given a verbal warning by the Director General
column16: He will be allowed in but must stand throughout the dinner
column18: All students are expected to be seated quietly in the Dining Hall by 4:30 p.m. latest as a requirement for keeping the term [1, 2].

column3: Segun attends his second dinner wearing a navy blue suit, a white shirt, and a stylish red bow tie. The dinner marshal stops him at the entrance. Why is Segun's attire improper?
column4: Navy blue is not considered a dark color
column5: He is missing a white breast pocket square
column6: The rules specifically prescribe a black tie and prohibit bow ties
column7: He is not wearing pinstripe trousers
column9: For male students, regulation dress includes a black tie, and the rules explicitly state "not bow tie" [3, 4].

column3: Amina is attending her law dinner and decides to wear a sleeveless dark dress with a white blouse underneath. Is this attire acceptable?
column4: Yes, as long as the dress is dark and she has a white blouse
column5: No, the dark jacket or dress must have long or three-quarters sleeves
column6: Yes, sleeveless is allowed for non-final dinners
column7: No, sleeveless dresses are only allowed if she wears a blazer over it
column9: Female students' regulation dress requires that the dark jacket or dress must be in long or three-quarters sleeves [2, 3].

column3: During the first course, Tunde uses his dinner knife to cut his bread roll into four equal quarters before buttering them. Which rule of etiquette has Tunde breached?
column4: Bread should be bitten directly, not cut or broken
column5: Bread rolls should be broken by hand into small pieces, not cut with a knife
column6: Bread should only be eaten after the soup is finished
column7: He should have used a specific bread knife, not the dinner knife
column9: Students are expected to use their hands to break the bread rolls into pieces that will conveniently go into the mouth [5, 6].

column3: While the main course is being served, Amaka realizes the salt shaker is on the other side of the table near a fellow student. She reaches across two other students to grab it. What is the correct procedure?
column4: She should have stood up to reach it
column5: She should have waited for a steward to bring it
column6: She should whisper to the colleague nearest to the item to pass it to her
column7: Stretching is permitted if done quickly and quietly
column9: Students should not stretch their hands across other diners to reach for things; rather, they should whisper to the colleague nearest to such items to pass them [6, 7].

column3: Emeka is excited to be dining with a famous Life Bencher. He discreetly takes a photo of the Bencher's procession using his silent-mode smartphone. What is the ethical position on this?
column4: It is allowed if the phone is on silent
column5: It is allowed because Life Benchers are public figures
column6: Photograph taking is strictly forbidden during dinner
column7: It is only allowed during the after-dinner speech
column9: Photograph taking is strictly forbidden during dinner and all mobile phones must be switched off [6, 8].

column3: During the loyal toast, a Bencher says, "May we toast to the prosperity of the Federal Republic of Nigeria. To the Federal Republic of Nigeria." What is the correct response from the students?
column4: "To the Federal Republic of Nigeria"
column5: "God bless the Federal Republic of Nigeria"
column6: "Prosperity to our nation"
column7: "Long live Nigeria"
column9: The prescribed response to the loyal toast is strictly "To the Federal Republic of Nigeria" [9, 10].

column3: A student, feeling unwell during the dinner, stands up and begins to walk toward the exit without speaking to anyone. Is this permissible?
column4: Yes, medical emergencies are automatic exceptions
column5: No, students are forbidden from moving about the hall unless the presiding Bencher allows it on representation
column6: Yes, but he must leave his student ID with the porter
column7: No, he must wait for the desert to be served before leaving
column9: Students are forbidden from moving about the hall unless in exceptional cases when the presiding Bencher allows on a representation made by the Director General or the Secretary [6, 8].

column3: For her final dinner before the Call to Bar, Bolatito wears her collaret and a white blouse. A senior lecturer informs her she is missing a required item. What is it?
column4: A white pocket square
column5: A dark veil
column6: A camisole to be worn over the collaret
column7: A silver brooch
column9: For the final dinner at call to Bar, a camisole is to be worn over the collaret [1, 2].

column3: After finishing his three-course meal, Tunde crosses his fork and knife in the middle of his plate. Is this the correct way to signal he has finished?
column4: Yes, crossing cutlery is the standard signal
column5: No, cutlery should be put together and placed at the side on the plate
column6: No, the fork should be on the left and the knife on the right
column7: No, he must leave the plate empty to signal completion
column9: When a student finishes eating, he indicates that by putting his cutlery together and placing them together at the side on his plate [6, 7].

column3: a practicing barrister sits at a table with five law students. The students decide to discuss a recent controversial court case in loud voices to impress the barrister. Is this appropriate? "
column4: Yes, law dinners are for intellectual discussion
column5: No, students should only speak to each other in very low tones
column6: Yes, provided the barrister joins the conversation
column7: No, students are not allowed to speak at all during the meal
column9: During dinner, students are allowed to speak to each other in very low tones only and must observe proper table manners [6, 8].

column3: When the Benchers and Barristers begin their procession into the hall at 5:00 PM, what is required of the students?
column4: They should remain seated and clap
column5: They should stand and remain standing until the Benchers take their seats
column6: They should bow as each Bencher passes
column7: They should continue eating their bread rolls quietly
column9: All students remain standing until all the Benchers file in and take their seats [6, 11].

column3: Fatimah, a devout Muslim, wishes to wear a hijab to the Law School dinner. What are the regulations regarding this?
column4: No headgear of any type is allowed
column5: Only black hijabs that are moderate in size are allowed
column6: Any color of hijab is allowed if it matches the suit
column7: Only short veils are allowed, not full hijabs
column9: Moderate black hijabs and short veils are now allowed for female students during dining terms [2].

column3: An aspirant to the Bar has passed all her Bar Part II examinations with a First Class but was found to have missed two out of the three compulsory dining terms due to an internship. Can she be called to the Bar? "
column4: Yes, academic performance overrides dining terms
column5: No, keeping 3 dining terms is a compulsory requirement for the Call to Bar
column6: Yes, if she pays a fine for the missed terms
column7: No, she must retake the entire year
column9: Students are expected to keep 3 dining terms, and their successful completion is a compulsory requirement for the Call to the Nigerian Bar [12-14].

column3: Before the soup is served, the presiding Bencher stands up to say grace. What should the students do?
column4: Remain seated and bow their heads
column5: Stand up while the grace is said
column6: Continue breaking their bread rolls
column7: Stand up only if they are of the same faith
column9: Before commencement of dinner, the presiding Bencher says the grace while everybody stands [5, 6].

column3: While eating the soup course, John uses his spoon to scoop the soup toward himself and makes a slight slurping sound. Which rule has he violated?
column4: Soup should be scooped away from the person
column5: He must not use a spoon for soup
column6: Students are expected to drink the soup gently without making noise
column7: Slurping is allowed if the soup is hot
column9: Students are expected to drink the soup gently with the spoon provided and must not make any noise at dinner [5-7].

column3: A female student attends the dinner wearing a dark skirt that ends 3 inches above her knees. Is this acceptable? "
column4: Yes, if she is wearing black tights
column5: No, there should be no miniskirts or uneven hemlines
column6: Yes, for the first two dinners only
column7: No, skirts must be floor-length
column9: There should be no miniskirts or uneven hemlines on the skirt or dress, and skirts should at least be knee length [1, 2].

column3: During the after-dinner speech, a student's phone, which was on vibrate, starts buzzing loudly on the wooden table. The student quickly hides it in his pocket. What is the correct protocol?
column4: Vibrate mode is acceptable
column5: All mobile phones must be switched off entirely
column6: Phones can be on if kept in a bag
column7: Only the ringing sound is prohibited
column9: The rule is strict that all mobile phones must be switched off during dinner [6, 8].

column3: At the end of the dinner, after the closing prayers, the Benchers begin to file out. When are students permitted to leave their positions?
column4: Immediately after the prayer is said
column5: Once the Benchers have left the hall and the student procession is called
column6: As soon as they finish their dessert
column7: After they have cleared their own plates
column9: Benchers and Barristers file out in procession while students remain silent and standing until the process is complete [10, 15].

column3: A male student arrives for dinner wearing a dark brown suit and a white shirt. He is denied entry. Why?
column4: Only black suits are allowed
column5: The rules prescribe dark clothing, and brown is generally not recognized as the standard professional dark attire (black, navy, or charcoal)
column6: He was missing a tie
column7: Brown is considered a "loud" color
column9: Students are expected to be in dark clothing; for males, regulation dress is typically black, blue-black, or charcoal grey suits [3, 4, 16].

column3: During a heated cross-examination at the High Court of Lagos, Mr. Emeka, a legal practitioner, tells the presiding judge, "Your Lordship is clearly biased and acting like a mouthpiece for the claimant." The judge immediately orders Mr. Emeka to be put in the dock to show cause why he should not be committed for contempt.
column4: Summary trial
column5: Full trial before another judge
column6: Referral to the NBA
column7: Police investigation
column9: For contempt committed in the face of the court (in facie curiae) that is notorious and incontestable, the judge has the option of a summary trial where the contemnor is put in the dock and asked to show cause. This is supported by the principle in Dibia v Ezeigwe and Section 6(6)(a) of the 1999 Constitution [1-3].

column3: A famous Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN) grants a live television interview regarding a high-profile criminal trial he is currently defending. During the interview, he argues that the evidence against his client is "fabricated junk" and that the judge has no choice but to acquit.
column4: Civil Contempt
column5: Criminal Contempt (Trial Publicity)
column6: Professional Negligence
column7: Mere Discourtesy
column9: Rule 33 of the RPC prohibits a lawyer from making extra-judicial statements likely to interfere with or prejudice the fair trial of a pending or anticipated matter. This is also known as the subjudice rule and constitutes criminal contempt [4-7].

column3: Following a judgment against his client, Mr. Kola writes a scathing article in a national newspaper accusing the judge of being an "incompetent liar who received a bribe from the opposition."
column4: Contempt in facie curiae
column5: Contempt ex facie curiae
column6: Procedural discourtesy
column7: Civil Contempt
column9: Contempt committed outside the court's precincts, such as publications in newspapers attacking a judge, is classified as contempt ex facie curiae. The procedure for punishment requires police investigation and trial before a different judge to ensure fair hearing as per Boyo v. AG Mid-West [8-10].

column3: A High Court in Kano grants an interim injunction restraining a construction company from further work on a disputed site. Their lawyer, Mr. Musa, advises the company to ignore the order and continue construction, claiming the injunction was "wrongly obtained."
column4: Criminal Contempt
column5: Civil Contempt
column6: Contempt in facie curiae
column7: Professional negligence
column9: Civil contempt is generally the disobedience to court orders, judgments, or processes involving a private injury. Advising a client to disobey a valid court order constitutes civil contempt as established in Awobokun v Adeyemi [1, 11, 12].

column3: During a summary trial for contempt in the face of the court, the judge orders the lawyer, Miss Ada, to enter the witness box and give evidence under oath about her conduct.
column4: The judge is correct as the witness box is the standard place for evidence.
column5: The judge is wrong because the contemnor should be put in the dock, not the witness box, to protect her right against self-incrimination.
column6: The judge is correct if the facts are contestable.
column7: Summary trial does not require putting the lawyer anywhere.
column8: The judge is wrong because the contemnor should be put in the dock, not the witness box, to protect her right against self-incrimination.
column9: In a summary trial for contempt, the contemnor must be put in the dock and asked to show cause. Compelling them into the witness box violates the right against self-incrimination under Section 36(11) of the 1999 Constitution as held in Deduwa v State [2, 13].

column3: A lawyer is convicted of criminal contempt under Section 133 of the Criminal Code in Lagos. What is the maximum term of imprisonment the court can impose?
column4: One month
column5: Three months
column6: Six months
column7: One year
column9: Under Section 133 of the Criminal Code of Lagos, criminal contempt carries a maximum punishment of three months' imprisonment [14-16].

column3: Mr. Benson, a lawyer, sends a private letter to a judge's residential address. In the letter, he explains that his client is a "good man" and offers a "contribution to the judge's upcoming vacation" if the judge rules in their favor.
column4: Mere discourtesy
column5: Criminal Contempt
column6: Civil Contempt
column7: Professional Negligence
column9: Every private communication to a judge for the purpose of influencing their decision upon a pending matter, regardless of whether a bribe is offered, is a criminal contempt as it interferes with the administration of justice according to Awobokun v Adeyemi [5, 17, 18].

column3: Mrs. Okafor, representing the defendant, arrives in court 45 minutes after the judge has delivered a final judgment in the matter. The judge, frustrated by her absence, cites her for contempt.
column4: Mrs. Okafor is guilty of criminal contempt.
column5: Mrs. Okafor is guilty of civil contempt.
column6: Her absence is a mere discourtesy/dereliction of duty and not necessarily contempt of court.
column7: Her absence is professional negligence but not contempt.
column8: Her absence is a mere discourtesy/dereliction of duty and not necessarily contempt of court.
column9: Persistent absence without leave can be contempt, but isolated absence or lateness is generally treated as a mere discourtesy or dereliction of duty, not contempt, as held in Izuora v Queen and Oku v State [19-21].

column3: To establish that a lawyer has breached an order of injunction (Civil Contempt), what is the required standard of proof?
column4: Balance of probabilities
column5: Preponderance of evidence
column6: Proof beyond reasonable doubt
column7: Reasonable suspicion
column9: Although civil in nature, the outcome of contempt is penal. Therefore, the standard of proof in both civil and criminal contempt is proof beyond reasonable doubt as established in Awobokun v Adeyemi [14, 22-24].

column3: A Magistrate in Abuja notices that a lawyer published an article in a newspaper the previous day describing the Magistrate as "corrupt and partial" regarding a case heard that morning. The Magistrate wants to punish the lawyer for contempt.
column4: The Magistrate can try the lawyer summarily.
column5: The Magistrate can order the lawyer's arrest for a full trial in his own court.
column6: The Magistrate has no jurisdiction to punish for contempt committed outside the court (ex facie).
column7: The Magistrate can strike the lawyer's name off the roll.
column8: The Magistrate has no jurisdiction to punish for contempt committed outside the court (ex facie).
column9: While every court has power to punish contempt in its face (in facie), a Magistrate Court has no power to punish for contempt committed outside the court (ex facie curiae) as per Section 6 of the Criminal Code and Nunku v IGP [2, 25].

column3: Mr. Yusuf, a lawyer, prepares an affidavit for his client to sign. The affidavit contains several paragraphs describing the presiding judge as a "notorious political hack" and "unfit for the bench."
column4: Only the client is liable for contempt as the deponent.
column5: Only the lawyer is liable for professional negligence.
column6: Both the client and the lawyer who prepared the contemptuous affidavit are guilty of contempt.
column7: Neither is liable if the statements are true.
column8: Both the client and the lawyer who prepared the contemptuous affidavit are guilty of contempt.
column9: A lawyer who prepares a contemptuous affidavit for a deponent is equally, if not more seriously, guilty of contempt of court as held in AG v Chief Samuel Taiwo Oredein [26, 27].

column3: A lawyer is found guilty of civil contempt for refusing to return a title deed as ordered by the court. The judge orders him to be kept in prison until he produces the document. What type of order is this?
column4: Summary sentence
column5: Committal until the contempt is purged
column6: Allocutus
column7: Discharge and caution
column8: Committal until the contempt is purged
column9: A court may order a contemnor to be imprisoned until they "purge" their contempt, meaning they comply with the original order they disobeyed. This is common in civil contempt cases like Ikabala v Ojosipe [14, 16, 28].

column3: During the delivery of a ruling, Mr. James, a lawyer, stands up and shouts, "This is a most unjust remark and a travesty of justice!"
column4: This is fair criticism of the judge.
column5: This constitutes criminal contempt in the face of the court.
column6: This is civil contempt because it relates to a ruling.
column7: This is professional negligence.
column8: This constitutes criminal contempt in the face of the court.
column9: Interrupting court proceedings by words or conduct that insult the court, such as calling a remark "unjust" during judgment, constitutes criminal contempt in facie curiae as held in R v Jordan and Atake v AG Federation [5, 29-31].

column3: A lawyer is accused of contempt ex facie curiae. The judge who was the subject of the alleged attack insists on presiding over the contempt trial to "defend the honor of his court."
column4: The judge is correct as he witnessed the honor being attacked.
column5: The judge is wrong because the principle of nemo judex in causa sua requires another judge to hear the matter.
column6: The judge can only hear it if it is civil contempt.
column7: The judge can hear it if the lawyer agrees.
column8: The judge is wrong because the principle of nemo judex in causa sua requires another judge to hear the matter.
column9: For contempt committed outside the face of the court (ex facie), the procedure must follow standard criminal rules, and a judge who has been personally attacked should not hear the case to satisfy the principles of fair hearing and natural justice (nemo judex in causa sua) as per Boyo v AG Mid-West [8, 10, 32].

column3: A lawyer believes a judge is acting with extreme partiality. Instead of addressing the press, the lawyer files a formal petition with the National Judicial Council (NJC).
column4: This constitutes criminal contempt for scandalizing the court.
column5: This constitutes civil contempt.
column6: This is the proper procedure under the Rules of Professional Conduct.
column7: This is professional negligence.
column8: This is the proper procedure under the Rules of Professional Conduct.
column9: Under Rule 31(2) of the RPC, a lawyer who has a proper ground for complaint against a judicial officer must make the complaint to the appropriate authorities (such as the NJC or the Chief Judge) rather than the press [4, 33].

column3: In a state where the Penal Code applies, what is the maximum imprisonment for criminal contempt?
column4: 3 months
column5: 6 months
column6: 9 months
column7: 1 year
column9: Under Section 155 of the Penal Code (applicable in Northern states), the punishment for criminal contempt is a maximum of 6 months' imprisonment or a fine or both [14-16].

column3: A lawyer is cited for civil contempt for failing to comply with a court order. The court issues a specific form asking him to "show cause" why he should not be committed to prison. Which form is this?
column4: Form 1
column5: Form 48
column6: Form 49
column7: Form 10
column9: In civil contempt proceedings for disobedience of a court order, Form 48 (Notice of Consequence of Disobedience) is issued first to the contemnor. If they still fail to comply, Form 49 (Notice to Show Cause) follows [32].

column3: A lawyer is caught appearing in court and signing documents while his name has been struck off the roll of legal practitioners for previous misconduct.
column4: He is only liable for professional negligence.
column5: He can be charged with the criminal offence of impersonation and also dealt with for contempt of court.
column6: He is exempt from contempt if he was once a lawyer.
column7: He can only be fined N100.
column8: He can be charged with the criminal offence of impersonation and also dealt with for contempt of court.
column9: Under Section 22 of the LPA, a person who is not a legal practitioner (including those struck off the roll) who practices or acts as one can be charged with impersonation and also dealt with for contempt of court [34-36].

column3: A lawyer publishes a scholarly article in a law journal critically analyzing a recent Supreme Court decision on land law, arguing the court misapplied the Land Use Act.
column4: This is criminal contempt for scandalizing the court.
column5: This is civil contempt.
column6: This is fair, civil, and accurate criticism which does not amount to contempt.
column7: This is professional misconduct.
column8: This is fair, civil, and accurate criticism which does not amount to contempt.
column9: There is room for fair, civil, and accurate criticism of a judge or judgment made bona fide, which does not constitute contempt as held in Okoduwa v State [1, 21].

column3: A lawyer is tried summarily for contempt in the face of the court. The judge finds him guilty and immediately sentences him to one month in prison without a separate charge being filed by the police.
column4: The judge followed the wrong procedure; police must always investigate.
column5: The judge is correct as summary trial for in facie contempt does not require a formal charge or police investigation.
column6: The judge can only fine him in a summary trial.
column7: The lawyer must be tried by the High Court if the judge is a Magistrate.
column8: The judge is correct as summary trial for in facie contempt does not require a formal charge or police investigation.
column9: For contempt committed in facie curiae, the court has the option of a summary trial where the judicial officer who witnessed the act punishes the contemnor immediately after giving them a chance to show cause, without the need for standard criminal prosecution procedures like police investigation [2, 3, 13, 37].

column3: During a murder trial, the defense counsel's car broke down on his way to the court. He sent a formal letter to the Judge and the prosecutor explaining the situation and requesting a 30-minute stand-down. The Judge refused the request and proceeded to allow the prosecution to lead its star witness in evidence while the counsel was absent.
column4: The Judge acted within his powers to manage the court's time efficiently.
column5: The Judge's action constitutes a breach of the constitutional right to fair hearing.
column6: The counsel is liable for professional negligence for being late.
column7: The prosecution has the right to proceed if the defense is not seated by 9:00 AM prompt.
column9: In Okoduwa v State, the court held that refusing a reasonable request for adjournment and proceeding without counsel breaches the constitutional provision of fair hearing under Section 36 of the 1999 CFRN [1], [2].

column3: Mr. Emeka, a legal practitioner, appeared before the High Court of Lagos State to represent a client. The presiding Judge, noticing that Mr. Emeka had not paid his practicing fees for the current year as of June 15th, refused to grant him a right of audience.
column4: The Judge is duty-bound to grant audience to any legal practitioner regardless of fee status.
column5: The Judge's refusal is proper because non-payment of practicing fees results in a loss of the right of audience.
column6: Only the NBA can stop a lawyer from appearing in court, not the Judge.
column7: The Judge should have allowed him to appear and ordered him to pay the fees the next day.
column9: Section 8(2) of the Legal Practitioners Act and Rule 9 of the RPC provide that a legal practitioner must pay his annual practicing fee to be accorded the right of audience in any court in Nigeria [3], [4].

column3: During cross-examination of a witness, the presiding Judge continually interrupted Counsel for the Defendant, dictating the specific questions to be asked and expressing his personal opinion on the witness's credibility.
column4: The Judge has the right to lead the proceedings to reach a faster conclusion.
column5: Counsel must obey the Judge's instructions on how to conduct his cross-examination.
column6: The Judge should not unduly interfere, as counsel must be allowed to conduct the case in the way he thinks best.
column7: The Judge is acting as a "Minister in the Temple of Justice" and is thus beyond reproach.
column9: In Jones v National Coal Board, it was established that the court should not interfere with how counsel conducts his case, as undue interference may lead to the judgment being set aside [1], [5].

column3: A High Court Judge, visibly irritated by an objection raised by a junior counsel, called the counsel "incompetent and stupid" in the presence of the litigants and the press.
column4: The Judge has the duty to maintain respect for counsel and avoid intimidation.
column5: Counsel must accept the insult as part of court discipline.
column6: The Judge is immune from any ethical duty regarding his language toward lawyers.
column7: The counsel should be cited for contempt for provoking the Judge.
column9: The court has a duty of respect toward counsel and is expected to avoid intimidating or insulting language. This was affirmed in Ezeogu v Onwuchekwa [6], [5].

column3: A legal practitioner appeared before a Magistrate in Lagos and addressed the court as "Your Worship." The Magistrate immediately stopped the proceedings and insisted that the counsel be penalized for an incorrect mode of address.
column4: The counsel used the correct mode of address for a Magistrate in Lagos.
column5: The counsel should have used "My Lord" for a Magistrate in Lagos.
column6: The correct mode of address for a Magistrate in Lagos is "Your Honour," and the court should have corrected him with dignity.
column7: Address is irrelevant to the duty of the court.
column9: According to Section 352 ACJL and Section 8 of the Magistrates' Courts Law of Lagos State, Magistrates in Lagos are addressed as "Your Honour," though the court still owes a duty of respect to counsel during correction [7], [8].

column3: At the close of evidence in a civil suit, the Judge informed both parties that he did not require final addresses and would proceed immediately to deliver judgment to save time.
column4: Final addresses are discretionary and the Judge can dispense with them.
column5: Counsel has a constitutional right to be allowed to address the court after the close of evidence.
column6: Only the claimant is entitled to a final address as of right.
column7: The Judge's action is proper if the evidence is already clear.
column9: Section 294(1) of the 1999 CFRN and advocacy principles provide that it is a constitutional right to be allowed to address the court after the close of evidence [9], [10].

column3: A Judge is presiding over a land dispute where his biological brother is the lead counsel for the Plaintiff. The defense counsel applies for the Judge to disqualify himself on grounds of likely bias.
column4: The Judge must disqualify himself to maintain the appearance of impartiality and fair hearing.
column5: The Judge can stay on the case if he promises to be fair to both sides.
column6: Only the CJN can order a Judge to disqualify himself in such a scenario.
column7: Relationship with counsel does not affect the duty of the court to counsel.
column9: Under the Code of Conduct for Judicial Officers and Section 36 CFRN, a Judge should disqualify himself where his impartiality may likely be questioned due to personal relationships [11], [12].

column3: Counsel for the Defendant attempted to call a witness who was present in court pursuant to a subpoena. The Judge refused to allow the witness to testify, stating that he had already heard enough evidence from the Defendant.
column4: The Judge has the power to limit the number of witnesses a party calls.
column5: The refusal to allow a party to call witnesses is a breach of the duty to grant a fair hearing.
column6: Only the prosecution can determine when enough evidence has been led.
column7: The Judge is acting to prevent "undue descending into the arena."
column9: The court has a responsibility toward a lawyer to allow him to call his witnesses; failing to do so breaches the constitutional provision of "fair hearing" under Section 36 CFRN [1], [2].

column3: A legal practitioner who is a salaried Director of "XYZ Ltd" appeared fully robed in the High Court to represent the company in a contractual dispute. The Judge refused to grant him audience in that capacity.
column4: The Judge's refusal is proper because a lawyer in salaried employment cannot appear as an advocate for his employer.
column5: The Judge breached the lawyer's right of audience under Section 8 of the LPA.
column6: A Director can always represent his own company if he is a lawyer.
column7: The Judge should have allowed him to appear if the case was pro bono.
column9: Rule 8(1) and 8(3) of the RPC provide that a lawyer in salaried employment or a Director of a company shall not appear as an advocate in court for his employer [13], [14].

column3: In a complex commercial litigation, the Judge noticed that the Plaintiff's counsel was struggling to articulate a basic point of law regarding the Statute of Frauds. The Judge intervened to clarify the legal position to ensure the case was decided on its merits.
column4: The Judge is unduly descending into the arena and should remain silent.
column5: A Judge is to be impartial but is also not to sit unconcerned where counsel's incompetence is likely to cause injustice.
column6: Intervention in counsel's argument is always a breach of fair hearing.
column7: The Judge should cite the counsel for professional negligence instead of helping.
column9: While a Judge must be impartial, he is not to sit unconcerned where counsel's incompetence is likely to cause injustice, as stated in Musa Omo Eleja v Bangudu [2].

column3: A lawyer arrived at the High Court for a hearing and found the front row empty. Being a lawyer of 3 years post-call, he sat in the front row. The Judge ordered him to move to the back, stating the front row is reserved.
column4: The Judge's order is a breach of the lawyer's right of audience.
column5: The Judge is correct as the front row is reserved for SANs and Life Benchers.
column6: Every legal practitioner is equal and can sit anywhere.
column7: Junior lawyers have the right to sit in the front if they arrive first.
column9: Rule 26(2) of the RPC on equality is subject to the rules of precedence; the front row is reserved for SANs and Life Benchers as per Section 6 of the LPA [15], [16].

column3: During a trial, a Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN) appeared alone to conduct a civil case in the High Court. The Judge refused to allow the trial to proceed until the SAN provided a junior counsel.
column4: The Judge is wrong because a SAN can appear alone in any court.
column5: The Judge is correct because a SAN shall not appear as counsel in any civil case before a superior court of record without a junior.
column6: The rule against appearing alone only applies in the Supreme Court.
column7: The Judge should have fined the SAN instead of stopping the trial.
column9: Rule 2(1) of the Senior Advocates of Nigeria (Privileges and Functions) Rules 1979 precludes a SAN from appearing in civil cases without a junior or another SAN [17], [18].

column3: A Judge discovered that the lawyer appearing before him was the same lawyer who had filed a petition against him to the National Judicial Council (NJC) in a previous matter. The Judge refused to hear the lawyer, citing "personal animosity."
column4: The Judge is right to protect his dignity.
column5: The Judge must still accord the lawyer a right of audience provided no legal disability exists.
column6: The lawyer should have been cited for contempt immediately.
column7: The Judge should have asked the client to change counsel.
column9: The court has a responsibility to grant a fair hearing and right of audience to counsel regardless of personal feelings, as the duty is to the institution of justice [1], [5].

column3: During a hearing of a motion, the Judge noticed that the counsel was wearing a dark suit but had forgotten his wig and gown. The Judge refused to hear him and ordered him to leave the bar.
column4: The Judge is correct as counsel must be properly robed in Superior Courts.
column5: The Judge should have proceeded as the suit is a dark color.
column6: Robing is only mandatory for the delivery of judgment.
column7: Only the NBA can enforce robing rules, not the court.
column9: Rule 45 of the RPC and court decorum rules provide that at the Superior Courts, counsel must appear robed in their wigs and gowns [19], [20].

column3: A High Court Judge was observed having a private lunch with the lead counsel for the Plaintiff in the middle of a contested trial. The defense counsel raised an objection.
column4: The Judge is free to associate with lawyers outside the courtroom.
column5: A Judge should be cautious and avoid personal relationships with lawyers that may affect the discharge of his duties impartially.
column6: The defense counsel should have been invited to the lunch to balance the relationship.
column7: This is only an issue if the Judge pays for the lunch.
column9: Rule 2(1) of the Code of Conduct for Judicial Officers provides that a Judge should avoid personal relationships with lawyers which may affect impartial discharge of duties [21], [12].

column3: An English-trained Barrister obtained a warrant from the Chief Justice of Nigeria to represent a client in a specific fraud case in Nigeria. At the hearing, the trial Judge refused to hear him, stating he is not on the Nigerian Roll of Legal Practitioners.
column4: The Judge is correct because only those on the roll can practice in Nigeria.
column5: The Judge is wrong because a person authorized by warrant under Section 2(2) of the LPA is entitled to practice for those specific proceedings.
column6: The warrant only allows him to act as a solicitor, not an advocate.
column7: He needs to be a Nigerian citizen to use the warrant.
column9: Section 2(2) of the Legal Practitioners Act allows the CJN to issue a warrant to an advocate from a similar legal system to practice for the purpose of particular proceedings [22], [23].

column3: A Judge presiding over a civil matter in the FCT High Court insisted on addressing a female legal practitioner as "My Learned Colleague" instead of the traditional address.
column4: The Judge is correct because all lawyers are equal.
column5: The correct address from a Judge to a legal practitioner is "My Learned Friend."
column6: The Judge should have addressed her as "Counselor."
column7: There is no rule governing how Judges address lawyers.
column9: In court decorum, while lawyers address each other as "My Learned Friend," the Judge also uses this term, reflecting the "Ministers in the Temple of Justice" principle [7], [24].

column3: During a heated argument over the admissibility of a document, the Judge threatened to send the counsel to prison immediately without a hearing if he spoke one more word.
column4: The Judge is exercising his summary power to punish contempt in facie curiae.
column5: The Judge's threat is an act of intimidation and a breach of the duty to counsel.
column6: Counsel has no right to argue once a Judge has made a preliminary remark.
column7: The counsel should have been put in the dock first to show cause.
column9: The duty of the court includes respect for counsel and avoiding intimidation. Summary contempt power must be used with caution and not for personal aggrandizement [25], [5].

column3: A Judge in a State High Court decided to conduct the entire trial in his private chambers because the courtroom's air conditioning was broken. Counsel for the Defendant objected, stating he wanted the trial in open court.
column4: The Judge is correct to prioritize the comfort of the parties.
column5: The court has a duty to conduct proceedings in public to ensure a fair hearing.
column6: Trials in chambers are the same as trials in open court.
column7: Only the Chief Judge can decide where a trial takes place.
column9: Section 36 of the Constitution requires proceedings to be held in public. A Judge should not move to chambers without legal justification as it may affect the appearance of a fair trial [26], [5].

column3: A lawyer who is a party in a civil suit (the Plaintiff) appeared in full robes to argue his own case. The Judge ordered him to de-robe before addressing the court.
column4: The Judge is wrong because a lawyer can always robe when appearing in court.
column5: The Judge is correct because a lawyer shall not wear his robes when conducting his own case as a party.
column6: The lawyer can robe only if he is representing a co-plaintiff.
column7: The Judge should have allowed him to robe but remain in the gallery.
column9: Rule 45(2)(b) and Rule 36(f) of the RPC provide that a lawyer shall not wear his robes when conducting his own case as a party to a legal proceeding in court [27], [28].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: Mr. Adewale, a legal practitioner, visits his high-profile client, Chief Emeka, at the Chief's private residence to take instructions for a new land matter because the Chief is "too busy" to visit the office. There is no physical infirmity or security threat involved. What is the ethical implication of Mr. Adewale's action?
column4: It is professional as it is a high-profile client
column5: It is professional as long as the Chief pays the appearance fee
column6: It is unprofessional conduct as a lawyer should not take instructions at a client's house without special circumstances
column7: It is professional since the Chief is a long-standing client
column9: Rule 22 of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer shall not call at a client's house or place of business for the purpose of giving advice or taking instructions, except in special circumstances or for some other urgent reason. "Busy schedule" does not constitute a special circumstance like infirmity or custody [1-3].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: During an initial interview with Madam Kofo regarding a breach of contract, Barrister Tunji fails to mention that the matter could be resolved through the Lagos Multi-Door Court House, preferring to file a writ of summons immediately. Has Tunji breached any ethical duty?
column4: No, because litigation is the primary duty of a lawyer
column5: Yes, he has a duty to inform the client of ADR options
column6: No, because ADR is only for matrimonial causes
column7: Yes, but only if the client asked for ADR
column9: Under Rule 15(3)(d) of the RPC 2023, a lawyer shall not fail or neglect to inform his client of the option of alternative dispute resolution (ADR) mechanisms before resorting to or continuing litigation on behalf of his client [4-6].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: Chief Okafor informs his lawyer, Chidi, during a private session that he intends to burn down his competitor's warehouse next week to "level the playing field" in their pending lawsuit. Chidi decides to warn the competitor and the police. Is Chidi's action ethically permissible?
column4: No, it violates the rule of lawyer-client privilege
column5: Yes, disclosure is permitted to forestall the commission of a crime
column6: No, unless the competitor is also Chidi's client
column7: Yes, but only if Chidi withdraws from the case first
column9: Rule 19(3)(c) of the RPC 2023 and Section 192(1)(a) of the Evidence Act provide exceptions to the rule of confidentiality, allowing a lawyer to reveal a client's confidence when it relates to the intention to commit a crime and disclosure is necessary to forestall it [7-9].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: Barrister Amaka is interviewing a client who is narrating a long
column4: complex story about a land dispute. Amaka allows the client to speak for 20 minutes without interruption
column5: only nodding and maintaining eye contact to encourage the flow. Which skill is Amaka primarily employing? "
column6: Active questioning
column7: Passive listening
column9: Analysis stage
column11: Passive listening involves paying attention to the client's story without interrupting, which allows the client to narrate the facts in their own words and feel at ease [10, 11].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: In the middle of an interview, a client reveals that the opposing party is Barrister Dele's biological father. Dele realizes this creates a conflict but believes he can still win the case. What is Dele's immediate ethical duty?
column4: Continue the case but don't tell the client to avoid anxiety
column5: Disclose the relationship to the client at the time of the retainer or as soon as it is discovered
column6: Only disclose if the father's lawyer finds out
column7: Ignore it as family ties do not affect professional judgment
column9: Rule 17(1) of the RPC 2023 @tipulates that a lawyer shall, at the time of the retainer, disclose to the client all the circumstances of his relations with the parties and any interest in the controversy which might influence the client in the selection of the lawyer [12-14].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: After a long interview, Barrister Segun concludes that his client's claim for defamation is completely barred by the Statute of Limitations and has no chance of success. He is worried the client will be angry if he says so. What should Segun do?
column4: File the case anyway and hope the other side doesn't notice
column5: Tell the client the case is strong to keep the retainer
column6: Inform the client that the claim is hopeless
column7: Advise the client to lie about the dates to bypass the limitation period
column9: Rule 14(2)(e) of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer shall inform his client where he considers the client's claim or defense to be hopeless [15-17].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: A lawyer's secretary
column4: while having drinks with friends
column5: mentions the details of a high-profile divorce case being handled in the firm. Is the lawyer ethically responsible for this breach of confidence? "
column6: No, the lawyer did not speak, the secretary did
column7: Yes, the lawyer must ensure employees preserve client secrets
column8: No, because the secretary is not a legal practitioner
column9: Yes, but only if the lawyer was present when the secretary spoke
column11: Rule 19(4) of the RPC 2023 and Section 193 of the Evidence Act provide that the duty of confidentiality extends to the lawyer's employees and associates, and the lawyer must exercise reasonable care to ensure they preserve the client's secrets [18, 19].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: During an interview, Barrister Aisha asks her client: "Did you sign the document on the 5th of May at the bank?" This question is an example of what?
column4: An open-ended question
column5: Leading question
column6: Closed-ended question
column7: Mirror question
column9: A closed-ended question is one that demands a specific response or a "yes/no" answer, often used to fill in gaps or clarify specific details [20, 21].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: A client instructs Barrister Obi to bribe a court official to ensure a case is assigned to a "favorable" judge. Obi explains this is illegal, but the client insists. What is Obi's required course of action?
column4: Comply with the client's instructions as the client is the boss
column5: Withdraw from the employment immediately
column6: Bribe the official but keep it a secret
column7: Only bribe if the amount is reasonable
column9: Rule 15(2) of the RPC 2023 states that where a client insists on a course of conduct that is illegal or a breach of the law, the lawyer shall withdraw his employment [22-24].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: Barrister Funmi is following the Avrom Sherr model of interviewing. She has finished listening to the client's narrative and is now asking follow-up questions to clarify ambiguities. Which stage is she in?
column4: Listening stage
column5: Questioning stage
column6: Advising stage
column7: Execution stage
column9: Avrom Sherr propounded three broad stages: Listening, Questioning, and Advising. The Questioning stage is where the lawyer fills in gaps and clarifies ambiguous statements [20, 25, 26].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: During the counseling stage, Barrister Oke identifies three options for his client: litigation, mediation, or doing nothing. The client chooses litigation despite Oke's preference for mediation. What should Oke do?
column4: Refuse to represent the client
column5: Force the client to choose mediation
column6: Respect the client's final decision provided it is within the law
column7: Sign a settlement agreement on the client's behalf anyway
column9: Counseling should be client-centered; the lawyer provides the options and legal implications, but the final decision rests with the client, provided the course of action is lawful [24, 27].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: Barrister Jude takes notes verbatim during a 2-hour client interview, writing down every single word the client says. What is the professional critique of this approach?
column4: It is the best way to ensure no facts are lost
column5: Verbatim note-taking is discouraged as it interferes with active listening and eye contact
column6: Verbatim notes are required by the RPC for all interviews
column7: It is professional only if the client is paying by the hour
column9: The sources state that note-taking should be of important points and not verbatim, as a human being cannot effectively listen and write verbatim simultaneously, and it distracts from the interview process [28, 29].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: A client comes to Barrister Joy for an interview but cannot pay the consultation fee immediately. Joy decides to take the instructions for free as the client is her elderly aunt. Is this ethically permissible? "
column4: No, undercutting is a professional misconduct
column5: Yes, a lawyer may charge no fee for a relative or indigent person
column6: No, every client must pay the scale fee
column7: Yes, but only if she reports it to the NBA
column9: While Rule 52(1) of the RPC 2023 prohibits undercutting, it provides an exception that a reduced fee or no fee at all may be charged on the ground of special relationship or the indigence of a client [30, 31].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: During an interview, Barrister Kazeem realizes that his firm is already representing the person his new client intends to sue. What is his duty?
column4: Take the new brief and assign it to a junior to avoid conflict
column5: Represent both and try to settle the matter
column6: Refuse the retainer due to conflict of interest
column7: Charge both clients double to compensate for the risk
column9: Rule 17(4) of the RPC 2023 states that a lawyer shall not accept a retainer if the exercise of his professional judgment will be affected by his own interests or if it conflicts with the interest of an existing client [12, 13].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: A client tells Barrister Tunde during an interview that he committed a murder three years ago for which someone else was wrongly convicted. The client does not intend to turn himself in. Can Tunde reveal this to the authorities? "
column4: Yes, because it is a serious crime
column5: No, it is a past crime protected by lawyer-client privilege
column6: Yes, but only with the Judge's permission
column7: No, unless the client is currently being tried for that murder
column9: Rule 19 of the RPC and Section 192 of the Evidence Act protect communications regarding past crimes. The exception for commission of a crime applies to future or ongoing crimes, not those already completed [7-9].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: Barrister Chidi uses highly technical Latin terms like "consensus ad idem" and "caveat emptor" throughout an interview with a rural farmer who has little formal education. Which skill has Chidi failed to apply effectively?
column4: Research skill
column5: Advocacy skill
column6: Verbal communication skill
column7: Drafting skill
column9: Effective verbal communication requires using proper, positive, and simple common words, avoiding legal jargon or "legalese" that the client may not understand [9, 32].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: According to the "Law School Version" of the stages of a client interview, what are the five stages?
column4: Listening, Questioning, Advising, Execution, Closing
column5: Preparation, Beginning, Client telling story, Analysis, Conclusion
column6: Starting, Gathering, Analyzing, Drafting, Ending
column7: Welcome, Facts, Law, Fees, Goodbye
column9: The Law School version uses the acronym PBCAC: Preparation for the interview, Beginning, Client telling the story, Analysis, and Conclusion [33].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: Barrister Mike is interviewing a witness of the opposing party in a civil matter without the consent of the opposing counsel. He advises the witness not to talk to the other side anymore. Is this ethical?
column4: It is ethical as it is a civil matter
column5: It is ethical because he doesn't need the other lawyer's consent
column6: It is unethical as he must not advise the witness to secrete themselves
column7: It is unethical because he must always get consent to talk to any witness
column9: Rule 25(1) of the RPC 2023 allows a lawyer to interview an opposing party's witness without consent, provided he does not hide the witness from the opposing party or advise the witness not to communicate with them [34, 35].
