column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: During the analysis stage of an interview, Barrister Bola summarizes her impression of the client's story and asks the client if she has captured everything correctly. This is an example of what type of question?
column4: Open-ended question
column5: Hypothetical question
column6: Mirror/Confirmatory question
column7: Leading question
column9: Mirror or confirmatory questions are used by the lawyer to summarize their impression and confirm that they have correctly understood the information divulged by the client [27, 36].

column2: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics
column3: Barrister Dare accepts a brief but, after the interview, discovers that the matter is in an area of law he has never practiced and knows nothing about. He decides to handle it alone to keep the entire fee. Is this ethical?
column4: Yes, because a lawyer is a "jack of all trades"
column5: No, he must associate with a competent lawyer unless the client objects
column6: Yes, as long as he does some research
column7: No, because he must only practice in one area
column9: Rule 16(a) of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer shall not handle a legal matter which he knows or ought to know that he is not competent to handle without associating with a lawyer who is competent to handle it, unless the client objects [30, 37, 38].

column3: Mr. Emeka, a legal practitioner, erects a large billboard in Ikeja with the caption: "Emeka & Co: We have won 95% of our cases in the last five years. Come to us for guaranteed victory."
column4: This is proper as it provides accurate information to the public
column5: This is improper as it includes a statement about the success rate of the lawyer's practice
column6: This is proper because lawyers are now permitted to advertise under the 2023 Rules
column7: This is only improper if Mr. Emeka is not a Senior Advocate of Nigeria
column9: Under Rule 39(2)(d) of the Rules of Professional Conduct (RPC) 2023, a lawyer is prohibited from engaging in any advertising which includes statements about the quality of his work, the size of his practice, or his success rate [1, 2].

column3: A lawyer, Mr. Kunle, visits a local land registry to search for defects in the titles of various properties. After finding several irregularities, he contacts the property owners and offers to represent them in litigation against the government.
column4: This is a proactive legal service and is professional
column5: This is improper as it constitutes instigating litigation and searching registries for defects with a view to employment
column6: This is proper if Mr. Kunle only charges a fixed fee
column7: This is only improper if the property owners are already represented by counsel
column9: Rule 47(1) and 47(2)(a) of the RPC 2023 provide that a lawyer shall not foment strife or instigate litigation, and specifically prohibits searching land registries for defects in title with a view to employment or litigation [3, 4].

column3: Barrister Bola enters into an agreement with a hospital ward attendant to pay him 10% of any professional fees earned from accident victims referred to her firm by the attendant.
column4: This is a valid referral fee agreement
column5: This is proper provided the client is informed of the arrangement
column6: This is improper as it involves offering rewards to persons to influence legal work and engaging agents to follow up on accidents
column7: This is only improper if the percentage exceeds 50%
column9: Rule 47(2)(c) and (d) of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from engaging agents to follow up on accidents or offering rewards to any person to influence legal work in favor of the lawyer [5, 6].

column3: Mr. Tunde, a legal practitioner, distributes handbills at a busy bus park in Lagos, listing his firm's address and claiming to be "The most affordable and efficient lawyer for all criminal matters."
column4: This is proper under the general permission for advertising
column5: This is improper solicitation of professional employment via handbills
column6: This is proper if the information on the handbills is accurate
column7: This is only improper if Mr. Tunde is not in full-time practice
column9: Rule 39(3)(a) of the RPC 2023 explicitly prohibits a lawyer from soliciting professional employment, either directly or indirectly, through the use of handbills, circulars, or advertisements [7, 8].

column3: A law firm, "Justice Seekers LLP," creates a website comparing their professional fees with three other prominent firms in the same locality, claiming their services are superior and cheaper.
column4: This is proper competition in a free market
column5: This is proper if the comparison is based on published NBA fee scales
column6: This is improper as it makes comparisons with or criticizes other lawyers
column7: This is proper provided the information is not misleading
column9: Rule 39(2)(c) of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer shall not engage in any advertising or promotion which makes comparisons with or criticizes other lawyers or other professions [1, 2].

column3: Following a major airplane crash, Barrister Chidi immediately sends letters to the grieving relatives of the victims, whom he does not know, offering his legal services to sue the airline for damages.
column4: This is a humanitarian act and is professional
column5: This is improper as it constitutes seeking out claimants in respect of personal injuries to gain employment
column6: This is proper if Barrister Chidi has expertise in aviation law
column7: This is only improper if Barrister Chidi charges a contingent fee
column9: Rule 47(2)(b) of the RPC 2023 prohibits a lawyer from seeking out claimants in respect of personal injuries or any other cause of action with a view to being employed by the prospective client [4, 5].

column3: Mr. Yusuf, a legal practitioner, publishes a brief biographical profile in a reputable Law Directory, including his name, address, schools attended, and his membership in the Nigerian Bar Association.
column4: This is a form of prohibited advertising
column5: This is improper solicitation
column6: This is a permitted form of advertisement
column7: This is only permitted for Senior Advocates of Nigeria
column9: Rule 39(4) of the RPC 2023 permits a lawyer to publish informative data about himself, including name, address, and academic qualifications, in a reputable law list or law directory [9, 10].

column3: A lawyer, Mrs. Ada, appears on a national television program to discuss general principles of landlord and tenant law. During the show, she offers to provide free private consultations to viewers who call her personal number displayed on the screen to discuss their individual rights.
column4: This is proper as it promotes legal education
column5: This is improper as she accepted employment to advise on individual rights during a public program
column6: This is proper provided she does not charge for the initial consultation
column7: This is only improper if she is robed
column9: Rule 46(1) of the RPC 2023 allows a lawyer to participate in media programs to give information on the law, but strictly prohibits accepting employment from such programs to advise on inquiries regarding individual rights [11, 12].

column3: Barrister Segun, a sole practitioner, uses a business card that describes him as "The Legal Lion: Specialist in Winning Hopeless Cases."
column4: This is proper as it showcases his specialty
column5: This is proper provided he has won at least one "hopeless" case
column6: This is improper as it is self-aggrandizing and misleading
column7: This is only improper if the card is distributed to non-clients
column9: Rule 39(2)(a) and (d) and 39(3)(e) of the RPC 2023 prohibit advertising that is misleading, includes statements about success rates, or constitutes self-aggrandizement [1, 9, 13].

column3: A lawyer, Mr. James, relocates his office to a new address. He sends a dignified notice of this change to his existing clients and also places a small advertisement of the change in a local newspaper.
column4: This is improper solicitation of new clients
column5: This is a permitted form of announcement
column6: This is only proper if he obtains permission from the NBA Branch Chairman
column7: This is improper because newspaper advertisements are strictly prohibited
column9: Rule 43 of the RPC 2023 provides that upon a change of address or telephone number, a lawyer may send a notice to his clients and may also insert an advertisement of such change in a newspaper or journal [12, 14].

column3: Barrister Musa, an expert in Intellectual Property law, sends a brief and dignified announcement of his availability to act as a consultant to other legal practitioners in his city.
column4: This is improper as he is soliciting from colleagues
column5: This is a permitted form of announcement under the Rules
column6: This is only proper if he is a Senior Advocate of Nigeria
column7: This is improper as it should only be sent to the NBA National Secretariat
column9: Rule 44 of the RPC 2023 permits a lawyer available to act as an associate or consultant to other lawyers to send a brief and dignified announcement of his availability to lawyers in his locality [12, 14].

column3: Mr. Okoro, a lawyer, puts up a sign outside his office building. The sign is 15 feet tall, features flashing neon lights, and includes a large photograph of himself in full legal robes.
column4: This is proper as it clearly identifies the office location
column5: This is improper as the sign is not of reasonable size and sober design
column6: This is proper provided he paid the local government signage tax
column7: This is only improper if the photograph was taken before he was called to bar
column9: Rule 41 of the RPC 2023 requires that signs or notices outside a lawyer's office must be of "reasonable size and sober design," and Rule 39(3)(c) prohibits procuring one's photograph to be published in connection with one's practice [13, 15, 16].

column3: While walking through a shopping mall, Barrister Jane notices a man arguing with a store manager. She approaches the man, hands him her visiting card, and tells him, "You have a great case for breach of contract. Call me and I will sue them for you."
column4: This is professional zeal to protect a citizen's rights
column5: This is improper solicitation by personal communication
column6: This is proper if she offers to handle the case pro bono
column7: This is only improper if the man is already represented by another lawyer
column9: Rule 39(3)(a) and Rule 47(1) of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from soliciting professional employment by personal communication or proffering advice to bring a lawsuit without being consulted [3, 7, 8].

column3: A legal practitioner, Mr. Bitrus, writes a scholarly article for a law journal on the "Evolution of Maritime Law in Nigeria." At the end of the article, he lists his name and his professional qualification as "LL.B, B.L, Maritime Law Consultant."
column4: This is improper advertising of his specialty
column5: This is proper and permitted under the Rules
column6: This is only proper if the journal is owned by the NBA
column7: This is improper because he should only list his name
column9: Rule 42 of the RPC 2023 provides that where a lawyer writes an article for publication giving information on the law, he may add his professional qualification after his name [12, 14].

column3: Barrister Effiong is representing a high-profile politician in an ongoing corruption trial. During a break in the proceedings, he holds a press conference on the court steps, claiming his client is "100% innocent" and that the judge's previous rulings in the case were "biased and legally illiterate."
column4: This is a proper exercise of the right to free speech
column5: This is improper as it involves making extra-judicial statements likely to interfere with a fair trial and brings the profession into disrepute
column6: This is proper if it is done to counter negative media reports about the client
column7: This is only improper if the press conference was paid for by the client
column9: Rule 33 and Rule 39(2)(b) of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from making extra-judicial statements likely to prejudice a fair trial or diminish public confidence in the legal profession [2, 17, 18].

column3: Barrister Sani, who is also a licensed real estate agent, runs a joint advertisement for his "Legal and Property Management Services," offering a discount on legal fees for any client who buys a house through his agency.
column4: This is proper business diversification
column5: This is improper as it involves practicing another profession simultaneously without GCB authority and is used to improperly attract business
column6: This is proper provided the legal fees do not fall below the 2023 Remuneration Order scales
column7: This is only improper if he is not a Senior Advocate of Nigeria
column9: Rule 7(1) of the RPC 2023 prohibits a lawyer from practicing another profession simultaneously with the law without authority from the General Council of the Bar, especially where it is designed to improperly attract business [22, 24].

column3: A lawyer, Miss Toyin, pays for a "Sponsored Post" on Instagram that features a video of her dancing in her wig and gown with a caption: "I am the baddest lawyer in town. My fees are the lowest, and I never lose. DM for your legal needs!"
column4: This is proper modern marketing
column5: This is improper solicitation and self-aggrandizement which brings the profession into disrepute
column6: This is proper as long as the "baddest lawyer" claim is subjective opinion
column7: This is only improper if she is not actually the "baddest lawyer"
column9: Rule 39(2)(b), (d) and 39(3)(e) of the RPC 2023 prohibit advertising that brings the profession into disrepute, includes claims about success, or involves self-aggrandizement [1, 2, 9, 13].

column3: Barrister John agrees to pay a police officer a fixed sum for every suspect the officer allows him to speak with at the police station for the purpose of securing a brief.
column4: This is a valid administrative facilitation fee
column5: This is proper if the suspect is actually in need of a lawyer
column6: This is improper solicitation through touts and influencers
column7: This is only improper if the suspect is eventually convicted
column9: Rule 39(3)(a) and Rule 47(2)(d) of the RPC 2023 prohibit soliciting through touts and offering rewards to persons who, by reason of their employment, can influence legal work [5-8].

column3: A law firm, "Global Law Chambers," prints its name, address, and the academic qualifications of its partners on its official envelopes and note-papers in a dignified font.
column4: This is a prohibited form of advertising
column5: This is proper and permitted under the Rules
column6: This is only proper if the firm has at least 10 partners
column7: This is improper because only the name of the firm is allowed
column9: Rule 40 of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer may cause to be printed on his note-papers, envelopes, and visiting cards his name, address, and academic and professional qualifications [15, 16].

column3: Colleagues and Profession ""
column4: Mr. Ade
column5: a legal practitioner
column6: wins a high-profile case and immediately posts a sponsored ad on Instagram stating his firm has a 99% success rate in commercial litigation to attract more clients. ""
column7: It is a permitted form of advertisement under Rule 39
column8: It is inaccurate but generally acceptable in modern practice
column9: it is improper because statements on success rates are prohibited
column10: It is allowed if the lawyer can provide evidence of the success rate
column12: Rule 39(2)(d) of the RPC 2023 prohibits a lawyer from including any statement about the quality of his work
column13: the size or success of his practice
column14: or his success rate in his advertising or promotion. [1]

column3: Lawyer A is aware that Mrs. X is already represented by Lawyer B in a land dispute, but Lawyer A proceeds to discuss a settlement offer directly with Mrs. X at her home without informing Lawyer B.
column4: It is acceptable since it is for the purpose of settlement
column5: It is a breach of duty to maintain good faith and fairness with a colleague
column6: It is allowed if the client invited Lawyer A to the meeting
column7: It is only allowed if the matter is non-contentious
column9: Under Rule 27(5) of the RPC 2023, a lawyer shall not have any dealing with a person represented by another lawyer in the same matter without giving prior notice to the other lawyer. [3], [4]

column3: Mr. Benson, a legal practitioner, hires a group of young men to hang around police stations and the High Court premises to redirect litigants and detainees to his law office for a commission.
column4: This is a creative way to ensure access to justice
column5: This amounts to soliciting professional employment through touts
column6: It is allowed if the touts are registered as paralegals
column7: It is permitted if the commission is reasonable
column9: Rule 39(3)(a) of the RPC 2023 prohibits a lawyer from soliciting professional employment either directly or indirectly through circulars, handbills, advertisement, or through touts. [1], [5], [6]

column3: A prosecutor, Mr. Idowu, realizes that a witness for the defense is telling the truth which confirms the innocence of the accused, but he chooses to suppress this information to ensure a conviction for the state.
column4: His primary duty is to procure a conviction for the State
column5: He is exercising his discretion as an advocate
column6: He has breached his primary duty to see that justice is done
column7: He should only disclose the information if the defense counsel makes a formal request
column9: Rule 37(6) of the RPC 2023 provides that the primary duty of a lawyer engaged in public prosecution is not to convict but to see that justice is done, and the suppression of facts establishing innocence is unethical. [7], [8], [9]

column3: Chief Emeka, a Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN), and Mr. Balogun, a Life Member of the Body of Benchers, appear in court for different mention matters. Who has precedence to mention his case first?
column4: Chief Emeka (SAN) takes precedence as the higher rank
column5: Mr. Balogun (Life Bencher) takes precedence over the SAN
column6: Precedence is determined by whoever arrived in court first
column7: The presiding Judge decides who speaks first based on the cause list
column9: By virtue of Section 6(3) of the LPA and Rule 26(2) of the RPC 2023, Life members of the Body of Benchers take precedence over all other legal practitioners, including Senior Advocates of Nigeria (SAN). [10], [11]

column3: Mr. John, a sole practitioner, decides to brand his law firm as "John, Obi & Co" to make his practice appear more established, even though Obi is not a lawyer.
column4: It is a smart marketing move for a new practitioner
column5: It is permitted if John plans to hire Obi as a clerk
column6: It is a breach of rules against misleading names and unauthorized partnership
column7: It is allowed as long as John is the only signatory to the account
column9: Rule 5(4) of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer practicing alone shall not use a firm name with the addition of "and Co" or any other name capable of holding out his practice as a partnership. [12], [13]

column3: A legal practitioner, Miss Amina, allows her non-lawyer senior clerk to draft a Deed of Mortgage and sign Amina's name on it to help a client who is in a hurry.
column4: Miss Amina has aided the unauthorized practice of law
column5: This is efficient delegation of administrative tasks
column6: It is allowed if Amina orally authorized the signature
column7: It is allowed if the clerk has a Diploma in Law
column9: Rule 3(1) and (2) of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from aiding a non-lawyer in the unauthorized practice of law or signing their name on a document prepared by a non-lawyer as if it were prepared by the lawyer. [14], [15]

column3: Lawyer A accepts a brief from a client who has just dismissed Lawyer B. Lawyer A takes the file and continues the case without ensuring that Lawyer B's outstanding professional fees are settled.
column4: Lawyer A has no professional obligation regarding Lawyer B's fees
column5: Lawyer A must use his best endeavors to ensure the former lawyer is paid
column6: The former lawyer must sue the client directly for his fees
column7: Only the court can direct a new lawyer to pay the old one
column9: Rule 29(3)(b) and Rule 27(4) of the RPC 2023 provide that a new lawyer accepting a brief shall use his best endeavors to ensure that all fees due to the former lawyer in the matter are paid. [3], [4], [16]

column3: During a civil trial, Lawyer Z finds a very recent Supreme Court decision that is directly against his client's case. He decides not to mention it, hoping the other side hasn't seen it.
column4: He is protecting his client's interest as a loyal advocate
column5: He has a duty to be candid and must disclose the authority to the court
column6: It is the sole duty of the opposing counsel to find unfavorable authorities
column7: He should only disclose the authority if the Judge specifically asks
column9: Rule 32(1) and Rule 37 of the RPC 2023 require a lawyer to be candid and fair as an officer of the court, which includes a duty to disclose decided cases that are against his client's position. [17], [18]

column3: Lawyer P knows that Lawyer Q represents the defendant. Since Q is ten minutes late for court and hasn't filed an appearance, P immediately moves the court to dismiss the defendant's counter-claim without calling Q.
column4: This is an efficient use of court time to favor his client
column5: This amounts to sharp practice and a lack of fairness to a colleague
column6: It is the defendant's fault for hiring a late lawyer
column7: This is required by the duty of dedication to the client
column9: Rule 27(2)(c) of the RPC 2023 states that when a lawyer knows the identity of the opposing counsel, he should not take advantage by causing a dismissal without first inquiring about the other lawyer's intention to proceed. [19], [20]

column3: Mr. Kayode, a legal practitioner, hears about a major gas explosion in a community and immediately goes there to distribute his business cards to the victims to encourage them to sue.
column4: He is providing necessary legal awareness to the community
column5: This is prohibited as instigating litigation and seeking out claimants
column6: It is a permitted form of advertisement for his specialization
column7: It is allowed if the victims are indigent and need pro bono help
column9: Rule 47(1) and (2)(b) of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from fomenting strife or instigating litigation and specifically from seeking out claimants in respect of personal injuries to gain employment. [21], [22], [23]

column3: Mr. Chidi is instructed by his client to bribe a court clerk to "lose" the file of a damaging witness so that the trial is delayed indefinitely.
column4: Chidi must follow his client's instructions to win the case
column5: Chidi must refuse and represent the client within the bounds of the law
column6: Chidi should only do it if the client provides a written indemnity
column7: Chidi should stay neutral and allow the client to handle the clerk
column9: Rule 15(2) and (3)(a) of the RPC 2023 state that a lawyer must keep strictly within the law notwithstanding contrary instructions and shall not give service involving corruption of public officers. [24], [25], [26], [27]

column3: Lawyer X is asked by Lawyer Y to hold his brief in a complex murder trial. Lawyer Y hands over the bulky case file to Lawyer X only five minutes before the Judge sits.
column4: Lawyer X should accept as a show of professional fellowship
column5: Lawyer X should not accept if he cannot acquire an adequate grasp of the matter
column6: Acceptance is mandatory because they are learned friends
column7: Lawyer X must accept and then ask for an adjournment in open court
column9: Rule 27(3) of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer shall not hand over a brief to another to hold, and the other shall not accept, unless it is handed over in reasonable time to acquire an adequate grasp of the matter. [3], [20]

column3: A Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN), Chief Williams, is personally instructed to appear in a Magistrate Court to argue a motion for bail for his childhood friend.
column4: He is demonstrating the dignity of the rank by helping a friend
column5: A SAN is generally prohibited from appearing in an inferior court
column6: He is allowed if it is a criminal matter and he is appearing alone
column7: He must appear with a junior or another SAN regardless of the court
column9: It is a restriction under the rules that a SAN shall not appear before an inferior court, which includes the Magistrate Court. [28], [29], [30]

column3: Mr. Tunde, a lawyer in full-time employment as a director in a Federal Ministry, maintains his private law firm, "Tunde & Associates," and acts as the Managing Partner.
column4: It is a legitimate way to manage his private affairs
column5: It is a breach of the Code of Conduct for Public Officers
column6: It is allowed if he does not personally appear in court
column7: It is permitted if he specializes in an area not related to his ministry
column9: The 5th Schedule of the 1999 Constitution prohibits a public officer employed on a full-time basis from engaging in the management of any private business, profession, or trade except farming. [31], [32], [33]

column3: Lawyer Bola writes a series of educational law articles for a legal journal and adds her LL.M and "Barrister-at-Law" titles after her name.
column4: This is improper self-promotion and advertising
column5: This is a permitted form of displaying her professional qualifications
column6: She should only include her name to avoid aggrandizement
column7: She must remove "Barrister-at-Law" as it is a qualification, not a title
column9: Rule 42 of the RPC 2023 permits a lawyer who writes a book or an article for publication to add his professional qualification after his name. [34], [35]

column3: Lawyer Greg, representing a high-profile defendant in a pending fraud case, holds a press conference to claim the Judge is "manifestly biased" and the trial is a "sham."
column4: This is his right to free speech on behalf of his client
column5: This is a breach of trial publicity rules and potential contempt of court
column6: It is allowed if the Judge actually appears biased
column7: This is permitted if the comments are made outside the court premises
column9: Rule 33 of the RPC 2023 prohibits extra-judicial statements likely to prejudice a fair trial, and Rule 31(2) requires complaints against judicial officers to be made to appropriate authorities, not the press. [36], [37], [38]

column3: A client decides to dismiss Lawyer A and hire Lawyer B mid-trial. Lawyer B promptly files a notice in court but tells the client not to speak to or pay Lawyer A.
column4: Lawyer B is being loyal to his new client
column5: Lawyer B has a duty to promptly give notice to Lawyer A
column6: Change of counsel only requires notification to the Registrar
column7: Notice is only required if the court orders it
column9: Rule 29(3)(a) of the RPC 2023 requires that when a client changes lawyer, the new lawyer shall promptly give notice to the former lawyer. [39], [40], [16]

column3: A lawyer, Mr. Silas, agrees to give 20% of his legal fees to a local community leader who referred a lucrative corporate client to his firm.
column4: It is a fair referral commission
column5: It is a breach of the rule against sharing fees with a non-lawyer
column6: It is allowed if the client is aware of the arrangement
column7: It is permitted because it promotes the lawyer's business
column9: Rule 3(1)(c) and Rule 53 of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from sharing his legal fees with a non-lawyer, as this aids in the unauthorized practice of law. [14], [41], [42]

column3: Lawyer Sani is in court and hears the court orderly read out an entry in the record of proceedings that is clearly wrong but favors Sani's client. Sani stays quiet to protect the advantage.
column4: He is fulfilling his duty of dedication to his client
column5: He has a duty to the state and court to ensure justice and must correct the record
column6: He should only correct it if the opposing counsel objects first
column7: It is the Judge's sole responsibility to ensure the accuracy of the record
column9: Rule 1 of the RPC 2023 states that a lawyer shall uphold the rule of law and promote the course of justice, which includes an obligation as an officer of the court to be candid and fair. [7], [43], [44], [45]

column3: Mr. Yusuf, a legal practitioner, is representing a client in a civil appeal. He finds a recent Court of Appeal decision that is directly against his client's position, but decides not to cite it because the opposing counsel is a young lawyer who has not cited it. What is the ethical implication?
column4: Yusuf has acted correctly by protecting his client's interest as a loyal advocate.
column5: Yusuf has a duty to be candid and must disclose the authority to the court.
column6: Yusuf should only disclose it if the Judge specifically asks.
column7: Yusuf should withdraw from the case to avoid misleading the court.
column9: Rule 32(1) and Rule 37 of the RPC 2023 require a lawyer to be candid and fair as an officer of the court, which includes a duty to disclose decided cases that are against his client's position. [1], [2]

column3: A client instructs his lawyer, Barrister Dele, to prepare a deed of assignment for a land transaction. The client explicitly tells Dele to "backdate" the document by two years to avoid a new tax law. Dele complies. What is the ethical implication?
column4: Dele has acted correctly by following his client's instructions.
column5: Dele has breached his duty to represent the client within the bounds of the law.
column6: Dele is protected by attorney-client privilege.
column7: Dele has only committed a minor clerical error, not misconduct.
column9: Rule 15(1) and (2) of the RPC state that a lawyer's first duty is to uphold the law and he must keep strictly within the law notwithstanding any contrary instruction from the client [3, 4].

column3: During a trial, a lawyer, Mr. Ade, notices that the court clerk has made an error in recording the previous day's proceedings that is highly favorable to his client. What is Mr. Ade's duty?
column4: He must remain silent and protect his client's advantage.
column5: He has a duty to the state and court to ensure justice and must correct the record.
column6: He should only correct it if the opposing counsel objects.
column7: He should withdraw from the case immediately.
column9: Rule 1 of the RPC 2023 states that a lawyer shall uphold the rule of law and promote the course of justice, which includes an obligation as an officer of the court to be candid and fair. [5, 6, 7]

column3: A legal practitioner, Mrs. Idowu, is representing a client in a civil suit. She forgets the trial date and fails to attend court, leading to the case being struck out. When the client sues her for negligence, she points to a clause in their retainer agreement that says, "the lawyer shall not be liable for any mistakes made during the case." Is this clause valid?
column4: Yes, parties are free to contract as they wish.
column5: No, any provision purporting to exclude or limit a lawyer's liability for negligence in a contract is void.
column6: Yes, but only if the client signed it before a Notary Public.
column7: No, unless the NBA President approved the waiver.
column9: Section 9(1) of the Legal Practitioners Act (LPA) provides that a person shall not be immune from liability for damages attributable to his negligence, and any contract provision purporting to exclude this liability is void [8, 9].

column3: A legal practitioner, Mr. Effiong, is representing a plaintiff in a land suit. Halfway through the trial, Effiong realizes that he was a witness to the original survey agreement and his testimony is essential to prove the case. What is the proper course of action?
column4: He should continue as counsel and also testify as a witness.
column5: He should withdraw from the case and let another lawyer handle the trial while he testifies as a witness.
column6: He should refuse to testify to protect the attorney-client relationship.
column7: He should ask the judge to decide based on his oral arguments without his testimony.
column9: Rule 20 of the RPC provides that a lawyer should not accept employment if he knows he or a lawyer in his firm may be a witness; if he discovers this during trial, he should withdraw from the case [10, 11].

column3: Chief Benson retains Barrister Amina for a civil litigation matter. After two hearings, Benson informs Amina that he is changing his lawyer to Barrister Kunle. Amina refuses to hand over the case file until her outstanding fees are fully paid. Is she within her rights?
column4: No, she must hand over everything to the new lawyer immediately.
column5: Yes, a lawyer has a lien on the papers or documents of his client in respect of unpaid fees.
column6: No, she can only withhold the client's original title deeds but not the correspondence.
column7: Yes, but only if she gets a court order to withhold the files.
column9: Under Rule 29(3)(c) of the RPC, the former lawyer is entitled to a lien on the papers or documents of his client in respect of unpaid fees [12, 13].

column3: During a trial, Barrister Ibrahim finds a recent Supreme Court decision that completely destroys his client's case. The opposing counsel is a junior lawyer who is unaware of this decision. What is Ibrahim's duty?
column4: He must remain silent and hope the other side doesn't find it.
column5: He must disclose the authority to the court, even though it is against his case.
column6: He should withdraw from the case immediately.
column7: He should tell the opposing counsel about it in private but not the judge.
column9: Rule 32 of the RPC (and Rule 37) requires a lawyer to be candid and fair; he must not fail to cite a decided case that is against him which has not been cited by the opposing counsel [1

column3: Barrister Dele is retained by a client in a property matter. While the matter is ongoing, Dele discovers that the client is using his law firm to launder money from an illegal drug business. What must Dele do to comply with current Nigerian law and ethics?
column4: He must keep the information secret due to attorney-client privilege.
column5: He must withdraw his services and report the suspicious activities to the appropriate authorities like the EFCC/NFIU.
column6: He should ask for a 10% cut of the laundered money as a "risk fee."
column7: He should only tell his local NBA Chairman.
column9: Under the Money Laundering (Prevention and Prohibition) Act 2022 and Rule 57 of the RPC 2023, lawyers have an obligation to report suspicious transactions to the appropriate authorities, and privilege does not cover transactions in furtherance of an unlawful act [15, 16, 17].

column3: A legal practitioner, Mr. Adams, is assigned a pro bono case by the Legal Aid Council. Adams decides not to prepare for the case or attend the trial since he is not being paid. What rule has he breached?
column4: Rule 24 on the Cab Rank Rule
column5: Rule 52 on undercutting
column6: Rule 14 on dedication and Rule 16 on competence
column7: Rule 7 on engaging in business
column9: Under Rule 14 and 16, a lawyer must devote expertise and energy to the service of his client; Section 9(1) of the LPA makes a lawyer liable for negligence regardless of whether services are provided for a fee or pro bono [8, 18, 19].

column3: Barrister Tolu collects 15 million Naira as a judgment debt on behalf of his client. Since he is currently renovating his own house and needs urgent cash, he deposits the money into his personal savings account, intending to pay the client back after his next big fee comes in. What duty has he breached?
column4: Duty of competence
column5: Duty of dedication
column6: Duty to deal with client's property honestly and separately
column7: Duty to accept brief
column9: Rule 23(2) of the RPC provides that a lawyer who collects money for his client shall not mix such money with his own and must report and account for it promptly [20, 21].

column3: Barrister Emeka receives a draft of ?5,000,000 from a judgment debtor in respect of a client's debt recovery case. He wants to open accounts to manage these funds. According to the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules, which accounts must he open?
column4: A personal current account and a personal savings account
column5: A Client's Account and an Office Account
column6: A joint account with the client
column7: A fixed deposit account in the client's name
column9: Under the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules, every lawyer in private practice must maintain a Client's Account for client money and an Office/Trustee Account for firm money [1, 2].

column3: What type of money is defined as ""Client's money"" under the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules?
column4: Money belonging to the lawyer's family
column5: Money held or received by a legal practitioner on account of a person for whom he is acting as a client
column6: Money earned as professional fees after the completion of work
column7: Money paid by the NBA as a welfare benefit
column9: "Client's money" is money held or received by a legal practitioner on account of a person for whom he is acting as a client, excluding money paid to him as a retainer or professional fees [3, 4].

column3: Which of the following may be paid into a Client's Account?
column4: Only money belonging to the legal practitioner
column5: Only client's money and trust money
column6: Only loans from a bank
column7: Only personal savings of the firm's partners
column9: Under Rules 3 and 4 of the Accounts Rules, only client's money, trust money, and money necessary to open or maintain the account may be paid into the Client's Account [5, 6].

column3: Barrister Musa is preparing to make a withdrawal from his Client's Account. For which of the following purposes can he legally withdraw money from the Client's Account?
column4: To pay his personal electricity bill
column5: To pay money due to a client or on the client's direction
column6: To buy a new car for his law firm's associate
column7: To pay for his child's school fees
column9: Under Rule 7 of the Accounts Rules, money may be withdrawn from a client's account for payments to or on behalf of the client, or for payment of a debt due to the practitioner from the client [7, 8].

column3: In which of the following banks can a legal practitioner open a Client's Account in Nigeria?
column4: Any commercial bank licensed by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN)
column5: Any microfinance bank in the local government
column6: Any foreign bank without a branch in Nigeria
column7: Only the Central Bank of Nigeria itself
column9: A Client's Account must be opened in a bank licensed as a commercial bank by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) [9, 10].

column3: What is the main purpose of keeping a Client's Account separate from the Office Account?
column4: To maximize interest earnings for the law firm
column5: To prevent the commingling of client funds with the lawyer's personal or business funds
column6: To avoid paying taxes to the Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS)
column7: To show the bank that the lawyer has many clients
column9: The fundamental purpose of keeping a separate Client's Account is to prevent the commingling of client's funds with the lawyer's personal or business funds, ensuring client money is protected [11, 12].

column3: If a client disputes the amount of professional fees a lawyer wants to withdraw from the Client's Account for completed work, what must the lawyer do?
column4: Withdraw the money anyway and let the client sue
column5: Leave the disputed portion in the Client's Account until the dispute is resolved
column6: Close the entire account and open a new one
column7: Pay the disputed money to the NBA Chairman
column9: Where there is a dispute regarding the lawyer's fees, the disputed portion must remain in the Client's Account and cannot be withdrawn until the dispute is resolved or taxed [13, 14].

column3: Who is responsible for auditing the accounts of a legal practitioner under the Accounts Rules?
column4: The Nigerian Bar Association President
column5: A qualified and independent Chartered Accountant
column6: The Auditor General of the Federation
column7: The bank manager of the commercial bank
column9: The accounts of a legal practitioner must be audited annually by a qualified and independent Chartered Accountant to ensure compliance with the Rules [15, 16].

column3: What is the consequence if a legal practitioner fails to comply with the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules?
column4: He will be fined ?100 by the local government
column5: He is liable to disciplinary action for professional misconduct before the LPDC
column6: His commercial bank account will be automatically closed by the CBN
column7: He will be banned from traveling abroad
column9: Non-compliance with the Accounts Rules constitutes professional misconduct and makes the legal practitioner liable to disciplinary action before the LPDC [17, 18].

column3: Which of the following is NOT ""Client's money""?
column4: Money received for a land purchase on behalf of a client
column5: Money paid as a security for court costs
column6: Money paid to a lawyer as an advance for court filing fees
column7: Money paid as an agreed and fully earned professional fee
column9: Agreed and fully earned professional fees belong to the lawyer and are not classified as client's money; they should be paid into the Office Account [3, 19].

column3: Barrister Kazeem is auditing his firm's accounts. He notices that he used ?200,000 from the Client's Account to temporarily pay the firm's office rent, but fully replaced the money two weeks later before the client found out. What is the status of Kazeem's action?
column4: It is proper since the money was fully replaced and no harm was done
column5: It is a serious professional misconduct and constitute breach of trust
column6: It is proper if Kazeem records the replacement as a loan
column7: It is proper if the client is a close relative of Kazeem
column9: Temporary use of client funds for personal or business purposes, even if replaced, is a breach of the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules and constitutes professional misconduct [1, 2].

column3: How long is a legal practitioner required to preserve his books of accounts
column4: ledgers
column5: and records of bank transactions under the Accounts Rules?
column6: At least one year
column7: At least three years
column9: Indefinitely
column11: Under the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules, all books of accounts, ledger cards, bank statements, and receipt duplicates must be preserved for at least six years [3, 4].

column3: In a situation where a legal practitioner acts as a sole trustee for a family estate, what type of bank account must he open to manage the estate's funds?
column4: A personal joint savings account with his spouse
column5: A Trustee/Trust Account separate from his Client's and Office Accounts
column6: A standard Client's Account
column7: A cash deposit box at the corporate office
column9: A legal practitioner acting as a sole trustee or co-trustee must maintain a separate Trust/Trustee Account under the Accounts Rules [5, 6].

column3: What is the primary ledger that a legal practitioner must maintain for every individual client?
column4: A general journal ledger
column5: A Client's Ledger showing receipts and payments for each client individually
column6: A cash book showing only total daily cash receipts
column7: A personal diary ledger
column9: A practitioner must maintain a Client's Ledger that clearly and individually shows all transactions, receipts, and payments for each client [7, 8].

column3: If a client requests a statement of his accounts showing how the funds deposited with the lawyer were utilized, within what time frame must the lawyer provide it?
column4: Within one year of the request
column5: Promptly or within a reasonable time upon request
column6: Only after the entire litigation or transaction has concluded
column7: Only if the client pays a statement fee
column9: Under the rules of professional conduct and accounts rules, a lawyer must promptly account for and report on client's property and funds upon request [9, 10].

column3: Which of the following is a valid method for drawing money from a Client's Account?
column4: A cash withdrawal over the counter by the firm's clerk
column5: A crossed cheque or bank transfer directly to the beneficiary
column6: An oral instruction to the bank manager
column7: A signed blank check given to the client
column9: Withdrawals from a Client's Account should be made via crossed cheques or direct bank transfers to ensure trackability and prevent unauthorized cash withdrawals [11, 12].

column3: What should a legal practitioner do if he receives a single cheque that contains both client's money and the practitioner's earned professional fees?
column4: Deposit the entire cheque into his Office Account first
column5: Deposit the entire cheque into the Client's Account first and then transfer the practitioner's portion promptly
column6: Split the cheque physically into two parts
column7: Ask the client to write two separate cheques before taking any action
column9: Under the Accounts Rules, a mixed cheque must be deposited into the Client's Account first, and the practitioner's portion must be transferred out promptly once determined [13, 14].

column3: Which of the following books is NOT mandatory for a law firm to maintain under the Accounts Rules?
column4: A Cash Book
column5: A Client's Ledger
column6: A Journal or Transfer Book
column7: A visitor register book
column9: While Cash Books, Client Ledgers, and Journals are statutory requirements under the Accounts Rules, a visitor register book is not [15, 16].

column3: Under what circumstance can a commercial bank freeze or debit a Client's Account to settle a personal loan owed to the bank by the legal practitioner?
column4: Whenever the legal practitioner defaults on his loan
column5: Only with the oral consent of the bank manager
column6: Never; a bank has no right of set-off or lien on a Client's Account for the lawyer's personal debts
column7: Only if the client is also a customer of the bank
column9: A bank has no right of set-off, charge, or lien on a Client's Account in respect of any personal liabilities or loans of the legal practitioner [17, 18].

column3: Who has the authority to inspect the books of accounts of a legal practitioner to verify compliance with the Accounts Rules?
column4: The President of the Customary Court
column5: The General Council of the Bar or the Disciplinary Committee
column6: The local police inspector
column7: The tax officer from the local government
column9: The General Council of the Bar or the LPDC has the authority to direct the inspection of a legal practitioner's books of accounts to ensure compliance [19, 20].

column3: Mr. Okafor wants to hire Barrister Zainab to handle his divorce suit. He asks her to charge her fees based on a percentage of the property she recovers for him during the settlement. Zainab agrees. Is this arrangement ethically permissible?
column4: Yes
column5: contingent fees are allowed in all civil cases in Nigeria.
column6: No
column7: a legal practitioner shall not enter into an agreement for a contingent fee in any matrimonial cause.
column9: provided the agreement is in writing and registered with the NBA.
column11: unless the judge approves the percentage before the trial starts.
column13: Under Rule 50(1) and (2) of the RPC, a lawyer is prohibited from entering into a contingent fee arrangement in any matrimonial cause or criminal defense [1, 2].

column3: What is the primary statutory instrument that regulates the charging of fees by legal practitioners for non-contentious business in Nigeria?
column4: The Rules of Professional Conduct (RPC) 2023
column5: The Legal Practitioners Remuneration Order (LPRO) 2023
column6: The Magistrates' Courts Rules
column7: The Evidence Act 2011
column9: The Legal Practitioners Remuneration Order (LPRO) 2023 is the primary statutory instrument that regulates fees for non-contentious businesses like land agreements, probate, and corporate drafting [3, 4].

column3: Barrister Kunle prepares a Deed of Assignment for a property worth ?50,000,000. He wants to know the appropriate scale of fees to charge. Under the Legal Practitioners Remuneration Order, how are scales of fees categorized?
column4: Scale I
column5: Scale II
column6: and Scale III
column7: Scale A
column9: and Scale C
column17: The Remuneration Order categorizes fees under Scale I (for preparation of instruments), Scale II (for conducting sales/purchases), and Scale III (for other non-contentious matters) [5, 6].

column3: A client agrees to pay Barrister Tunde a fee that is 30% lower than the minimum fee prescribed under Scale I of the Remuneration Order. Tunde accepts the brief because he is desperate for work. What is this practice called?
column4: Under-charging
column5: Undercutting
column6: Pro bono service
column7: Fee-splitting
column9: Undercutting is the practice of charging fees lower than the minimum prescribed scale in the Remuneration Order, and it constitutes professional misconduct under Rule 52 of the RPC [7, 8].

column3: When is a legal practitioner legally entitled to sue his client for recovery of his professional fees? "
column4: Immediately after the work is completed
column5: After the expiration of one month from the date of delivery of a signed Bill of Charges to the client
column6: Only after obtaining a judgment from the Magistrate Court
column7: Whenever the client refuses to pay an oral demand
column9: Section 16 of the LPA provides that a legal practitioner can only sue for his fees after the expiration of one month from the date on which a signed Bill of Charges was delivered to the client [9, 10].

column3: What essential document must a legal practitioner deliver to his client before he can commence a lawsuit to recover unpaid fees?
column4: A Writ of Summons
column5: A Letter of Demand
column6: A signed Bill of Charges
column7: An affidavit of urgency
column9: Under Section 16(1) of the LPA, a legal practitioner must serve his client with a signed Bill of Charges before he can commence any action in court to recover his fees [10, 11].

column3: What does ""taxation of bills"" mean in the context of legal fees in Nigeria?
column4: The payment of income tax on legal fees earned
column5: The process where a court official (the Taxing Master) reviews and determines the reasonableness of a lawyer's bill of charges
column6: The deduction of tax at source by the client
column7: A special tax levy on high-profile law firms
column9: "Taxation" of bills is the formal process where a court-appointed Taxing Master reviews and determines the reasonableness of a legal practitioner's bill of charges [12, 13].

column3: Which of the following is NOT a permissible method of charging fees for contentious business in Nigeria?
column4: A fixed fee / Lump sum retainer
column5: An hourly rate
column6: A contingent fee in a criminal defense case
column7: A retainer fee paid periodically
column9: A contingent fee in a criminal defense case is strictly prohibited under Rule 50(2) of the RPC and is therefore not permissible [2, 14].

column3: Barrister Kemi agrees to represent a client in a debt recovery suit. They agree in writing that Kemi's fee will be 15% of whatever amount is successfully recovered from the debtor. Is this agreement valid?
column4: Yes, this is a valid contingent fee agreement for a civil contentious matter.
column5: No, contingent fees are strictly illegal in Nigeria.
column6: Yes, but only if the client pays a 5% registration fee to the NBA.
column7: No, unless the debtor also agrees to pay the fee.
column9: Rule 50(1) of the RPC permits contingent fees in civil contentious matters, provided the agreement is in writing, reasonable, and not in matrimonial or criminal cases [15, 16].

column3: What is a ""Retainer"" in the context of legal practice?
column4: A device used to keep files organized
column5: An agreement where a client pays a lawyer to secure his services over a period of time or for a specific series of matters
column6: A fee paid to a junior lawyer for doing research
column7: A penalty paid by a client for changing lawyers
column9: A retainer is an agreement where a client pays a legal practitioner to secure his services over a specified period or for a specific series of matters, ensuring the lawyer's availability [17, 18].

column3: A client is dissatisfied with the Bill of Charges delivered by his lawyer and believes the fees are grossly inflated. To whom should the client apply for the taxation of the bill?
column4: The President of the Nigerian Bar Association
column5: The Taxing Master (usually the Registrar of the High Court)
column6: The Chairman of the Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Committee
column7: The Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS)
column9: An application for taxation of a Bill of Charges is directed to the Taxing Master, who is typically the Registrar of the High Court of the State [1, 2].

column3: Within what time frame can a client apply for the taxation of a Bill of Charges after receiving it without needing a court order?
column4: Within one week
column5: Within twelve months (one year) of delivery
column6: Only within thirty days of delivery
column7: Anytime within five years
column9: Section 17 of the LPA provides that an application for taxation of a bill may be made within twelve months of delivery of the bill [3, 4].

column3: What is the effect of a taxed Bill of Charges?
column4: It is sent to the FIRS for tax computation
column5: It has the effect of a judgment of the court and can be enforced directly
column6: It is only a non-binding recommendation
column7: It must be approved by the NBA NEC before enforcement
column9: Once a Bill of Charges is taxed and a certificate is issued, it has the force of a judgment of the court and can be enforced directly without a fresh trial [5, 6].

column3: Barrister Adams delivers a Bill of Charges to a client. The client pays the bill in full. Six months later, the client discovers that the fee charged was double the legal scale and wants to tax the bill. Can the bill still be taxed?
column4: No, once a bill is paid, it can never be taxed.
column5: Yes, the court can still order taxation under special circumstances even after the bill has been paid, provided the application is within 12 months.
column6: Yes, but only if the lawyer agrees to the taxation.
column7: No, unless the lawyer is first convicted of a crime.
column9: Under Section 18 of the LPA, a bill can still be taxed after payment under special circumstances, provided the application is made within 12 months [4, 7].

column3: Which of the following is a key element that must be included in a valid Bill of Charges under Section 16 of the LPA?
column4: A detailed list of the lawyer's academic qualifications
column5: The names of all the partners in the law firm
column6: A clear statement of the items of work done and the specific charges for each item
column7: The bank account number of the NBA
column9: A valid Bill of Charges must contain a clear statement of the specific items of work done and the corresponding fees, and must be signed by the practitioner or a partner [8, 9].

column3: Under the Legal Practitioners Remuneration Order 2023, what is the consequence if a lawyer charges a fee below the minimum scale for preparing a deed of transfer?
column4: The deed of transfer becomes automatically invalid and void
column5: The lawyer is guilty of the offence of undercutting and liable to disciplinary action
column6: The bank will refuse to process the payment
column7: The land registry will refuse to register the deed
column9: Charging below the minimum scale constitutes the offence of undercutting under Rule 52 of the RPC, which is a professional misconduct [10, 11].

column3: If a lawyer delivers a Bill of Charges and the client files an application for taxation, can the lawyer commence an action to recover the fees while the taxation is pending?
column4: Yes, he can sue to recover the undisputed portion.
column5: No, no action to recover the fees can be commenced or continued while the taxation of the bill is pending.
column6: Yes, provided he gets permission from the local NBA Chairman.
column7: No, unless the client is about to flee the country.
column9: Section 16 of the LPA bars any action for recovery of fees while the taxation of the Bill of Charges is actively pending [12, 13].

column3: What is a ""Contingent Fee"" agreement?
column4: An agreement where the fee is payable only in the event of a successful outcome of the case
column5: An agreement where the fee is paid in monthly installments
column6: An agreement where the fee is determined by the size of the lawyer's office
column7: An agreement where the client pays the lawyer in properties rather than cash
column9: A contingent fee agreement is one where the fee is payable only in the event of a successful outcome or recovery in the case [14, 15].

column3: Under the Legal Practitioners Remuneration Order 2023, what are the geographical zones used to determine the applicable scale of charges?
column4: Northern Zone and Southern Zone
column5: Zone 1 (High density/commercial centers like Lagos and Abuja) and Zone 2 (Other states)
column6: East
column7: West
column9: and South
column14: LPRO 2023 divides charges into geographical zones based on commercial density, with Lagos and Abuja placed in the highest scale zone [16, 17].

column3: Who pays the cost of the taxation proceedings if the Taxing Master reduces the lawyer's bill by more than one-sixth of the original amount?
column4: The client
column5: The legal practitioner
column6: The Nigerian Bar Association
column7: The High Court registrar
column9: Under the standard rules of taxation, if the bill is reduced by more than one-sixth upon taxation, the legal practitioner pays the costs of the taxation proceedings [18, 19].

column3: Barrister Kemi receives a draft of ?10,000,000 on behalf of her client, but refuses to hand it over or report it to the client. After three months of silence, the client wishes to file a complaint. To which body should the client submit the petition?
column4: The National Executive Committee of the NBA
column5: The Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Committee (LPDC)
column6: The Body of Benchers
column7: The Disciplinary Committee of the local NBA Branch
column9: Complaints against a legal practitioner for professional misconduct are submitted to the LPDC (or through the NBA which forwards it to the LPDC) for investigation and trial [1, 2].

column3: A young lawyer is accused of "infamous conduct in a professional respect." Which of the following acts is most likely to be classified as infamous conduct by the LPDC?
column4: Filing a motion for adjournment on medical grounds
column5: Commingling client's funds with personal funds and refusing to account for them
column6: Charging a fee that is slightly higher than the average fee in his area
column7: Arriving 10 minutes late for a court hearing
column9: Infamous conduct in a professional respect includes actions like commingling and misappropriating client's funds, which violate the RPC and LPA and destroy trust [3, 4].

column3: What is the primary function of the Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Committee (LPDC)?
column4: To make rules of professional conduct
column5: To consider and determine cases where it is alleged that a person has been guilty of professional misconduct in his capacity as a legal practitioner
column6: To issue annual practicing licenses to lawyers
column7: To determine who is qualified to be appointed as a Senior Advocate of Nigeria
column9: Section 11 of the Legal Practitioners Act (LPA) establishes the LPDC with the primary function of considering and determining cases of alleged professional misconduct [5, 6].

column3: Who is the Chairman of the Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Committee (LPDC)?
column4: The Chief Justice of Nigeria
column5: A member of the Body of Benchers appointed by the Body
column6: The President of the Court of Appeal
column7: The Attorney-General of the Federation
column9: The LPDC is a committee of the Body of Benchers, and its Chairman is a Bencher appointed by the Body of Benchers [7, 8].

column3: If the LPDC finds a legal practitioner guilty of professional misconduct, which of the following directions can it legally make under the LPA?
column4: It can direct that the practitioner's name be struck off the roll
column5: It can sentence the practitioner to a term of imprisonment
column6: It can order the police to seize the practitioner's office building
column7: It can strip the practitioner of his university law degree
column9: Section 12(1) of the LPA provides that the LPDC can direct that the name be struck off the roll, suspend the practitioner, or admonish them [9, 10].

column3: Barrister Dele is found guilty of professional misconduct by the LPDC, which directs that he be suspended from private practice for 3 years. Dele is highly dissatisfied and wants to appeal. To which court does the appeal lie?
column4: The Court of Appeal
column5: The Supreme Court of Nigeria
column6: The Federal High Court
column7: The General Council of the Bar
column9: Under Section 12(7) of the LPA, an appeal from any direction of the LPDC lies directly to the Supreme Court of Nigeria [11, 12].

column3: Within how many days must a legal practitioner file his appeal against a direction of the LPDC?
column4: Within 14 days
column5: Within 28 days
column6: Within 90 days
column7: Within 120 days
column9: Section 12(7) of the LPA provides that an appeal against a direction of the LPDC must be filed within 28 days from the date of the direction [13, 14].

column3: A lawyer's name was struck off the roll in 2018 for financial dishonesty. In 2024, he wishes to apply for restoration of his name to the roll. To which body should he direct his application for restoration?
column4: The Nigerian Bar Association National Executive Committee
column5: The Body of Benchers
column6: The Supreme Court of Nigeria
column7: The Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Committee
column9: The Body of Benchers has the statutory power to consider applications for the restoration of a legal practitioner's name to the roll after disciplinary action [15, 16].

column3: What is the legal status of a direction of the LPDC before the period for filing an appeal has expired?
column4: It is immediately enforceable and cannot be stayed
column5: It remains suspended and does not take effect until the time for appeal has elapsed or the appeal is determined
column6: It is only a non-binding opinion
column7: It must be published in the national newspapers immediately
column9: A direction of the LPDC does not take effect until the 28-day period for appeal has elapsed or, if an appeal is filed, until the appeal is finally determined or withdrawn [17, 18].

column3: Which of the following bodies is responsible for receiving petitions against lawyers and conducting preliminary investigations before forwarding them to the LPDC?
column4: The Body of Benchers
column5: The Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Committee itself
column6: The Nigerian Bar Association (through its Disciplinary Committee)
column7: The High Court Registry
column9: The NBA, through its national or branch disciplinary committees, receives petitions, conducts preliminary investigations, and forwards prime facie cases to the LPDC [19, 20].

column3: Barrister Funmi maintains a personal blog where she posts detailed and opinionated commentary on ongoing high-profile trials in Nigeria, arguing that several judges are receiving bribes from political parties. What is the ethical status of her blog?
column4: It is a proper exercise of her freedom of expression and academic freedom.
column5: It is professional misconduct as it constitutes a breach of the rule against trial publicity and brings the profession into disrepute.
column6: It is proper provided she does not use her real name.
column7: It is proper if she is not representing any of the parties in the trials she discusses.
column9: Rule 33 of the RPC on trial publicity prohibits lawyers from making extra-judicial statements that are likely to prejudice a fair trial, and Rule 39(2)(b) prohibits promotions that bring the profession into disrepute [1, 2].

column3: Mrs. Zainab, a legal practitioner, wants to host a weekly podcast on Instagram where she will discuss general legal issues like consumer rights, tenancy, and employment law. She will not discuss active court cases or offer advice on individual disputes. Is this permissible?
column4: No
column5: all social media activity by lawyers is strictly prohibited.
column6: Yes
column7: this is a permitted form of public legal education
column8: provided she does not use it to solicit for individual cases.
column9: Yes
column10: but only if she obtains a permit from the NBA President.
column12: unless she is a Senior Advocate of Nigeria.
column14: Rule 46(1) of the RPC allows lawyers to participate in media programs to give information on the law, but they must not use it to accept employment to advise on individual rights [3, 4].

column3: Barrister Segun pays a social media influencer with 1 million followers on Twitter to post a tweet: "If you need the best and cheapest lawyer for your land or business registrations, DM Barrister Segun now!"
column4: This is proper modern digital marketing.
column5: This is professional misconduct as it involves soliciting professional employment through touts or public advertisements.
column6: This is proper as long as the influencer has a valid business license.
column7: This is proper provided Segun's fees do not fall below LPRO scales.
column9: Rule 39(3)(a) of the RPC explicitly prohibits a lawyer from soliciting professional employment, either directly or indirectly, through the use of touts, advertisements, or public media promotions [5, 6].

column3: A lawyer, Miss Toyin, posts a video of herself in full legal robes dancing on TikTok to celebrate a recent court victory, with the caption "We won! Another major victory in court today!"
column4: This is proper professional celebration.
column5: This is improper as it is self-aggrandizing and brings the profession into disrepute under the RPC.
column6: This is proper if she does not disclose the name of the client.
column7: This is only improper if she was not actually called to the bar.
column9: Rule 39(2)(b), (d) and 39(3)(e) of the RPC 2023 prohibit advertising that brings the profession into disrepute, includes claims about success, or involves self-aggrandizement [1, 7].

column3: A law firm, "Justice League Partners," creates a professional LinkedIn page listing their practice areas, office address, and the publications of their partners.
column4: This is a prohibited form of digital advertising.
column5: This is a permitted and proper form of informative data publication.
column6: This is only permitted if the firm pays a social media fee to the NBA.
column7: This is improper because law firms are not allowed to have social media pages.
column9: Rule 39(4) of the RPC 2023 permits a lawyer to publish informative data about himself or his firm, including names, addresses, and academic qualifications, on directories and professional platforms [8, 9].

column3: Barrister Chidi, an experienced legal practitioner, decides to form a partnership with Mr. John, a qualified and licensed Chartered Accountant, to offer a "one-stop shop" for legal and tax services. Is this partnership valid under the RPC?
column4: Yes
column5: multi-disciplinary partnerships are allowed under modern business laws.
column6: No
column7: a lawyer shall not form a partnership with a non-lawyer if any of the activities of the partnership consists of the practice of law.
column9: provided the legal and accounting services are kept in separate rooms.
column11: unless Mr. John also holds a Diploma in Law.
column13: Rule 5(1) of the RPC prohibits a lawyer from forming a partnership with a non-lawyer if any of the activities of the partnership consists of the practice of law [1, 2].

column3: A legal practitioner, Mr. Sola, wants to employ a qualified and licensed foreign lawyer as an associate in his Lagos law firm to handle international commercial agreements. Is this permissible?
column4: Yes
column5: there are no restrictions on employing foreign associates.
column6: Only with the express approval and consent of the Attorney-General of the Federation (AGF) or under reciprocity rules.
column7: Yes
column8: but only if the foreign lawyer is a citizen of a Commonwealth country.
column9: No
column10: a foreign lawyer can never work in a Nigerian law firm under any condition.
column12: The employment or practice of foreign lawyers in Nigeria is strictly regulated and subject to the provisions of the LPA and GATS/reciprocity frameworks administered by the AGF [3, 4].

column3: A multinational auditing firm, "ABC Accounting," hires Barrister Kemi as a salaried legal officer. Her duties include drafting contracts and representing ABC's corporate clients in tax disputes before the High Court. What is the ethical status of Kemi's work?
column4: It is proper as she is a qualified and called legal practitioner.
column5: It is improper as she is aiding a non-lawyer (the accounting firm) in the unauthorized practice of law and appearing as an advocate for an employer.
column6: It is proper if the corporate clients sign a waiver.
column7: It is only proper if the accounting firm pays her practicing fees.
column9: Rule 3(1) of the RPC prohibits a lawyer from aiding a non-lawyer in the unauthorized practice of law, and Rule 8(1) prohibits a salaried employee from appearing as an advocate in court for his employer [5, 6].

column3: Barrister Tunde is representing a client in a land transaction. He agrees to split his professional fees with a local estate agent who helped negotiate the price. Is this ethical?
column4: Yes
column5: fee-splitting is a standard real estate practice.
column6: No
column7: a lawyer shall not share his legal fees with a non-lawyer
column9: Yes
column10: provided the fee-sharing agreement is in writing.
column12: unless the estate agent is also a registered surveyor.
column14: Rule 3(1)(c) and Rule 53 of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from sharing his legal fees with a non-lawyer, as this aids in the unauthorized practice of law [7, 8].

column3: A law firm, "Apex Chambers," wants to hire a registered medical doctor as a full-time consultant to help analyze medical reports in personal injury cases.
column4: This is proper
column5: provided the doctor does not practice law or appear as an advocate.
column6: This is a prohibited form of partnership with a non-lawyer.
column7: This is only proper if the doctor has a law degree.
column8: This is improper because law firms cannot employ non-lawyers in any capacity.
column9: A
column10: A law firm can employ non-lawyer experts (like doctors, engineers, or accountants) as consultants or employees to assist the firm, provided they do not practice law or act as legal practitioners [9, 10].

column3: A British barrister, Mr. Arthur, is hired by a major oil company to represent them in an international commercial arbitration held in Lagos, Nigeria. The arbitration is governed by Nigerian law. Can Mr. Arthur appear as counsel?
column4: No
column5: only lawyers called to the Nigerian Bar can appear in any legal proceeding in Nigeria.
column6: Yes
column7: a foreign lawyer can act as counsel in arbitration proceedings in Nigeria
column8: as arbitration is not restricted to locally admitted lawyers.
column9: Yes
column10: but only if he obtains a special warrant from the Chief Justice of Nigeria.
column12: unless he is accompanied by a Senior Advocate of Nigeria.
column14: Arbitration is generally open to foreign legal practitioners under international frameworks, and the restriction on practicing as a legal practitioner in Nigeria primarily applies to litigation before courts of law [1, 2].

column3: An American attorney, Miss Sarah, wants to open a branch of her New York law firm in Abuja to advise clients on New York corporate law. Sarah has not been called to the Nigerian Bar.
column4: Sarah can open the office since she is only advising on New York law.
column5: Sarah cannot open or practice law in Nigeria without being called to the Nigerian Bar
column6: as private practice is restricted to enrolled practitioners.
column7: Sarah can open the office if she partners with a Nigerian lawyer who owns 51% of the firm.
column8: Sarah can practice if she obtains a special permit from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.
column9: B
column10: Section 2 of the LPA restricts the right to practice as a legal practitioner in Nigeria to persons called to the Nigerian Bar and enrolled on the Supreme Court roll [3, 4].

column3: Under what statutory framework can a foreign lawyer be admitted to practice law in Nigeria for a specific transaction or case?
column4: By applying for a temporary work visa from the Nigerian Immigration Service
column5: By obtaining a special warrant from the Chief Justice of Nigeria under Section 2(2) of the LPA
column6: By paying a registration fee to the General Council of the Bar
column7: By obtaining a recommendation from the President of the Court of Appeal
column9: Section 2(2) of the LPA allows the CJN, if satisfied that it is expedient to do so, to issue a warrant to a foreign legal practitioner to practice for the purpose of a particular proceeding [5, 6].

column3: A Nigerian lawyer, Mr. Wale, forms a partnership with a French law firm, "Paris Juris LLP," to offer cross-border legal services. Wale resides in Lagos and manages the Lagos office, while the French partners manage the Paris office. Is this permissible?
column4: No
column5: international partnerships are strictly prohibited.
column6: Yes
column7: provided the partnership is conducted in accordance with the LPA and rules on partnership
column8: and the French partners do not practice in Nigeria without qualifying.
column9: Yes
column10: but only if all the French partners are called to the Nigerian Bar.
column12: unless the partnership is registered with the Corporate Affairs Commission (CAC) as a public company.
column14: International or cross-border partnerships are permissible provided they comply with local regulations, and the foreign partners do not engage in local practice without meeting the enrollment requirements [7, 8].

column3: When a foreign legal practitioner is granted a special warrant by the Chief Justice of Nigeria to represent a client in a specific case, what is the scope of his right to practice?
column4: He can practice in all courts and handle any case for a period of one year.
column5: His right to practice is strictly limited to the specific proceedings or case for which the warrant was granted.
column6: He can only act as a solicitor but cannot appear in court as an advocate.
column7: He can only practice if he is accompanied by the Attorney-General of the Federation.
column9: Section 2(2) of the LPA limits the warrant to the specific proceedings and any appeal arising therefrom, meaning it is not a general license to practice [6, 9].
